exercises.name: Barbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an adjustable bench to a 30-45° incline and position it inside a power rack or with a spotter and place the barbell on the rack at a height you can reach while seated.
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 30-45° incline and position it inside a power rack or with a spotter and place the barbell on the rack at a height you can reach while seated.
Sit with your back and head firmly against the bench, feet flat on the floor, and brace your core (tighten abs and glutes) to maintain a stable, slight arch in the lower back.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists stacked over elbows and shoulders pulled down and back.
Unrack the bar by straightening your arms and moving the bar directly above your upper chest/shoulder line; have a spotter if needed.
Inhale, bend the elbows, and lower the bar under control to the upper chest/clavicle area (touch point just below the top of the sternum) keeping elbows at roughly 30–60° from the torso.
Pause briefly at the bottom without bouncing, ensuring the forearms remain vertical and the core stays braced.
Exhale and press the bar back up in a slightly backward arc until the arms are fully extended but not locked aggressively at the elbows.
Repeat for the desired repetitions, then carefully re-rack the bar on the uprights with control or have the spotter assist.
exercises.description: The barbell incline bench press is a compound pressing movement performed on an inclined bench that emphasizes the upper portion of the pectorals while also engaging the anterior delts and triceps. It is done from a supine position on an incline bench, pressing a barbell vertically from chest to full arm extension.

exercises.name: Barbell Feet Flat Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set the bench inside a rack so the bar sits at arm height when you lie down; load the bar and secure collars or have a spotter.
instructions: Set the bench inside a rack so the bar sits at arm height when you lie down; load the bar and secure collars or have a spotter.
Lie supine on the flat bench with your eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and planted for stability, and a slight natural arch in the lower back.
Retract and depress your shoulder blades (pinch them together and press them into the bench) to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the bar with a full grip slightly wider than shoulder width, wrists stacked over forearms and elbows under the bar.
Unrack the bar by straightening your arms and moving the bar into the start position directly above your shoulders.
Inhale, then lower the bar in a controlled manner to mid-chest / nipple line, keeping elbows at about a 45° angle to the torso and maintaining scapular retraction.
Briefly pause with the bar lightly contacting the chest (do not bounce), then exhale and press the bar upward along a slightly arced path back to full arm extension.
Lock out the elbows at the top without overextending, keep the feet planted and core braced throughout the set.
After the final rep, carefully rack the bar onto the supports or have your spotter assist; use a spotter for heavy loads and maintain strict control on every rep.
exercises.description: Barbell feet-flat flat bench press is a supine pressing exercise that primarily works the pectorals by pressing a loaded barbell from the chest to full arm extension. Keeping the feet flat provides a stable base while the triceps and delts assist in elbow extension and shoulder stabilization.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Squeeze Bench Press
exercises.notes: Step 1: Sit at the end of a flat bench and bring two dumbbells together so their heads touch; lie back so you are supine with feet flat on the floor and knees bent.
instructions: Step 1: Sit at the end of a flat bench and bring two dumbbells together so their heads touch; lie back so you are supine with feet flat on the floor and knees bent.
Step 2: Hold the dumbbells together at mid-chest with palms facing each other (neutral grip) and actively squeeze the dumbbells together to create tension in the chest and serratus anterior.
Step 3: Brace your core and keep a slight natural arch in the lower back (do not over-arch); keep the dumbbells touching and elbows tucked to about a 30–45° angle from the torso.
Step 4: Exhale and press the dumbbells upward in a controlled motion until the arms are nearly extended while maintaining the inward squeeze; avoid fully locking the elbows.

Step 5: Pause briefly at the top while continuing to squeeze the dumbbells together, then inhale and slowly lower them back to the starting position at the mid-chest, maintaining contact between the dumbbells.
Step 6: Repeat for the desired number of reps, focusing on constant squeeze and controlled tempo; stop or reduce weight if form breaks or shoulders begin to compensate.
exercises.description: A supine dumbbell press variation where two dumbbells are held together and squeezed throughout the movement to increase chest activation and inner-pec engagement. Press the paired dumbbells up from the chest while maintaining constant inward pressure.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Lever Incline Hammer Chest Press
exercises.notes: Set the bench to an incline (about 30–45 degrees) and select an appropriate weight on the lever machine.
instructions: Set the bench to an incline (about 30–45 degrees) and select an appropriate weight on the lever machine.
Sit on the incline bench with your back firmly against the pad, feet flat on the floor, and hips stable.
Reach up and grasp the neutral (hammer) handles with a thumb-around grip so palms face each other; hands should start near the upper chest/shoulder line.
Retract your shoulder blades slightly and brace your core to maintain a stable torso throughout the lift.
Exhale and press the handles upward and slightly forward in a controlled arc until your arms are nearly straight but not locked out.
Pause briefly at the top with tension on the pectorals, then inhale and slowly lower the handles back to the start position under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, keeping wrists neutral and avoiding excessive flaring of the elbows or arching of the lower back.
exercises.description: Seated incline chest press using a neutral (hammer) grip on a lever machine that emphasizes the upper chest. The movement presses the handles upward and slightly forward from the level of the upper chest to near full arm extension.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45° and sit with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45° and sit with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
Lie back so your upper back and head are supported (supine on the incline bench) and plant your feet flat on the floor for stability.
Bring the dumbbells up to shoulder level with palms facing forward and elbows bent (upper arms roughly 45° from the torso) while retracting the shoulder blades slightly.
Unrack the dumbbells by pressing them up and then lowering them to start position so the handles are beside your upper chest/shoulder area.
Press the dumbbells upward and slightly together until your arms are nearly straight (avoid locking or hyperextending the elbows); exhale during the press.
Pause briefly at the top with tension in the pectorals, then slowly lower the dumbbells under control back to the upper chest/shoulder level while inhaling.
Keep a stable core and avoid excessive arching of the lower back; maintain scapular control and smooth, controlled reps for the desired number of repetitions.
If using heavy weights, have a spotter or perform a controlled seated dumbbell rack/unrack to avoid strain when starting or finishing the set.
exercises.description: A pressing movement performed lying supine on an incline bench that emphasizes the upper portion of the chest. Dumbbells allow a slightly greater range of motion and independent arm movement compared with a barbell.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Alternative Fly
exercises.notes: Set an adjustable bench to a 30–45° incline and sit with a dumbbell in each hand.
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 30–45° incline and sit with a dumbbell in each hand. Plant your feet flat on the floor and lie back so your head, upper back, and butt are supported (supine on incline bench).
Hold the dumbbells with a neutral or slightly pronated grip and press them together over your upper chest with a small bend in both elbows; this is the start position.
Keep your shoulder blades lightly retracted and braced throughout the set to protect the shoulder joint.

Lower one dumbbell in a slow, controlled arc out to the side (maintain the same elbow bend) until you feel a stretch in the upper chest; keep the opposite arm stationary and slightly elevated above the chest.
Pause briefly at the bottom for tension on the pectorals, then drive that dumbbell back up in the same arc to meet the elevated dumbbell above the chest, squeezing the pectorals at the top.
Alternate sides each repetition, maintaining control, a constant elbow angle, and avoiding bouncing or letting the lowered elbow drop behind the plane of your torso.
Breathe in as you lower the weight and exhale as you bring it back up. Use a weight that allows strict form and full control for the full range of motion.
Avoid shrugging the shoulders or overarching the lower back; stop immediately if you feel sharp shoulder pain and reduce range or load.
exercises.description: Lying supine on an incline bench, perform alternating single-arm dumbbell flyes in a wide arc to isolate the upper chest (pectorals) while one arm remains elevated as the other lowers. The movement emphasizes a controlled stretch and contraction of the upper chest with stabilization from the anterior delts and forearms.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Fly
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30-45 degrees and sit with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30-45 degrees and sit with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
Lie back (supine on an incline bench) and plant your feet flat on the floor for stability; bring the dumbbells up to shoulder height with palms facing each other and a slight bend in the elbows.
Retract and depress your shoulder blades slightly (pinch the shoulder blades together) to stabilize the chest and protect the shoulders.
Begin the repetition by slowly lowering the dumbbells out to your sides in a wide arc, keeping the same slight elbow bend throughout; lower until you feel a comfortable stretch in the upper chest.
Keep the movement controlled and avoid dropping the weights below the plane of the chest or allowing the elbows to fully lock or flare excessively.
Drive the dumbbells back up by horizontally adducting the shoulders (bringing the arms together) until they meet or nearly meet above the sternum; exhale as you lift.
Pause briefly at the top with tension in the pectorals, then inhale and repeat for the desired reps.
Maintain a neutral neck and avoid shrugging the shoulders; use a weight that allows strict form and full control through both eccentric and concentric phases.
exercises.description: Performed lying back on an inclined bench, the dumbbell incline fly isolates the upper portion of the chest by moving the arms in a wide arc from open to together while maintaining a slight elbow bend. It emphasizes pectoral horizontal adduction with shoulder stabilization from the delts and grip support from the forearms.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.
instructions: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.

Lie supine on the bench with your head, upper back and glutes in contact with the bench, feet flat on the floor and a small natural arch in the lower back; retract and depress your shoulder blades to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists should be stacked over the elbows and forearms vertical when the bar is over the chest.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar out over the chest while keeping the scapulae stable; lock the elbows briefly before starting the set.
Inhale and lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-chest/sternum (around nipple level), keeping the elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso to protect the shoulders.
Allow the bar to touch or come very close to the chest without bouncing; maintain tension in the pectorals and keep the feet and glutes braced.
Exhale and press the bar upward explosively while keeping the bar path slightly arced toward the hips until the arms are fully extended but not hyperextended.
Pause briefly at the top with the shoulders stable, then begin the next rep. Maintain consistent form and breathing throughout the set.
Use a spotter or safety pins in the rack for heavy sets, and avoid flaring the elbows excessively or letting the lower back lift off the bench.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a compound pressing movement performed lying supine on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and anterior delts. It involves lowering a loaded barbell to the chest and pressing it back up to full arm extension with controlled technique.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie back so you are supine on the bench, feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and maintain a neutral spine with a slight natural arch; retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together.
instructions: Sit on the edge of a flat bench with a dumbbell resting on each thigh.
Lie back so you are supine on the bench, feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and maintain a neutral spine with a slight natural arch; retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together.
Use your legs and core to help bring the dumbbells up to chest height so your upper arms are about 45 degrees from your torso and palms are facing forward (or slightly inward if preferred).

Inhale, then slowly lower the dumbbells in a controlled manner until your elbows are roughly level with the bench or just below chest level, keeping wrists neutral and elbows tracking slightly tucked.

Pause briefly at the bottom without relaxing the shoulder blade position, then exhale and press the dumbbells upward and slightly together until your arms are almost fully extended — avoid locking the elbows.
Control the descent back to the starting position and repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
To finish the set, bring the dumbbells safely to your knees, sit up, and set them down or have a spotter assist if using heavy weight.
exercises.description: The dumbbell bench press is a supine pressing movement performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and delts for elbow and shoulder extension. Dumbbells allow a greater range of motion and require more stabilization from the forearms and serratus anterior.

exercises.name: Lever Incline Fly
exercises.notes: Adjust the machine so the back pad is set to an incline (approximately 30–45°) and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Adjust the machine so the back pad is set to an incline (approximately 30–45°) and select an appropriate weight.
Sit with your back pressed firmly against the pad, feet flat on the floor, chest lifted and shoulders down; grasp the handles with a neutral grip and arms out to the sides at roughly chest height.
Slightly bend your elbows and retract/depress your shoulder blades to stabilize the shoulders before starting the movement.
Exhale and drive the handles upward and inward in a controlled, wide arc, leading with the elbows while maintaining the slight elbow bend.
Bring the handles together above the upper chest and hold a brief squeeze (about 1 second) focusing on contracting the pectorals.

Inhale and slowly reverse the motion, resisting the weight as you lower the handles back to the starting position until you feel a controlled stretch in the chest—do not let the shoulders shrug or the elbows fully lock.
Repeat for the prescribed number of repetitions, keeping tempo controlled and avoiding momentum or excessive range that causes shoulder discomfort.
exercises.description: Seated incline lever fly performed on an incline lever machine to isolate the upper portion of the pectorals by moving the arms in a wide arc while the torso is supported on an incline pad.

exercises.name: Barbell Wide Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench under a rack and load the barbell; position yourself lying supine on the bench with eyes roughly under the bar.
instructions: Set up a flat bench under a rack and load the barbell; position yourself lying supine on the bench with eyes roughly under the bar.
Plant feet firmly on the floor, pull your shoulder blades together and down (retract and depress) and maintain a tight core and neutral spine.
Grip the bar with a wide, pronated grip (wider than shoulder-width) so the forearms are more angled when the bar is at chest level.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over your chest, keeping shoulders stable and the elbows under the bar.
Inhale, slowly lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-chest / nipple line while keeping the elbows tracking out at a moderate flare and the scapulae retracted.
Pause briefly with the bar lightly touching the chest (do not bounce), then exhale and press the bar back up along a slight arc until the arms are nearly extended.
Avoid locking the elbows aggressively at the top, maintain tension in the pectorals and triceps, and keep the wrists neutral.
Return the bar to the rack with control after your set; use a spotter if lifting heavy or approaching failure.
exercises.description: A barbell bench press performed with a wider-than-shoulder grip to emphasize the outer pectoral fibers. Lying supine on a flat bench, the bar is lowered to the mid-chest and pressed back to full arm extension.

exercises.name: Cable Standing Chest Press
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to about chest/shoulder height and attach single hand grips; select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to about chest/shoulder height and attach single hand grips; select an appropriate weight.

Stand in the middle of the station facing away from the stacks with one foot slightly forward (staggered stance) for stability, arms bent and handles held at chest level, elbows roughly at chest height.
Brace your core (engage abs), keep a neutral spine and shoulders slightly retracted to set the starting position; wrists remain neutral.
Step forward or press your feet into the floor to create tension in the cables so the handles cannot move without effort.
Exhale and press both handles forward in a controlled motion, extending the arms until nearly straight but without locking the elbows; keep the movement horizontal and drive from the chest.
At full extension, pause briefly and squeeze the pectorals (and serratus anterior) while keeping the torso stable and not overextending the lower back.
Inhale and slowly return the handles to the start position with control, allowing the elbows to bend to chest level while maintaining core tension.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, keeping consistent tempo and avoiding excessive leaning or rotating of the torso.
exercises.description: A standing cable press performed from a dual‑pulley station that emphasizes horizontal pushing to work the chest. The movement also requires shoulder and triceps involvement while the core stabilizes the torso.

exercises.notes: Set an adjustable bench to a 30-45° incline and position it inside a power rack or with a spotter and place the barbell on the rack at a height you can reach while seated.

exercises.name: Cable Alternative Fly
exercises.notes: Set two cable pulleys at a low or mid position (roughly at or slightly below chest height when standing) and attach single-handle grips.
instructions: Set two cable pulleys at a low or mid position (roughly at or slightly below chest height when standing) and attach single-handle grips.
Place a flat bench centered between the cable towers and lie supine (on your back) with feet flat on the floor and head supported by the bench.
Reach up and grab one handle in each hand with a neutral or palms-facing grip; start with the arms out to the sides and elbows slightly bent (soft elbows).
Retract and stabilize your shoulder blades on the bench to minimize shoulder elevation and protect the spine.
Exhale and bring one hand (or alternate hands) in a smooth arc toward the center of your chest, maintaining the same slight elbow bend and keeping the motion driven by the pectorals.
Pause briefly at the peak contraction with the hands meeting or slightly overlapping at midline, squeezing the chest.
Inhale and slowly return the working arm to the start position under control, resisting the cable's pull; keep torso and hips stable and avoid excessive arching of the lower back.
Repeat for the desired reps, alternating arms if performing the alternating variation; keep tempo controlled and avoid using momentum.
exercises.description: A supine cable fly performed on a flat bench where each arm works alternately to bring the handle across the chest, emphasizing horizontal adduction of the shoulder to target the pectorals. The exercise uses a slight elbow bend and controlled tempo to isolate the chest.

exercises.name: Cable Low Fly
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single-hand handles.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single-hand handles.
Stand centered between the towers in an upright position with feet shoulder-width apart or one foot slightly staggered for stability.
Grasp the handles with a neutral grip so the cables run low behind your legs; step forward slightly to create tension in the cables and keep a tall chest.
Keep a slight bend in the elbows throughout the exercise (soft elbows) and a small forward lean from the hips; do not round the shoulders.
Exhale and pull both handles upward and inward in a controlled wide arc until your hands meet or nearly meet at chest level, squeezing the pectorals at the end of the motion.
Hold the peak contraction briefly, keeping the shoulders down and scapulae stable (use serratus anterior to stabilize the shoulder blades).
Inhale and slowly reverse the movement, allowing the cables to return your arms to the start position with control — do not let the weights slam.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining posture, a consistent elbow angle, and steady tempo throughout.
exercises.description: Standing cable low fly performed with the pulleys set low; you pull each handle upward and inward in a wide arc to bring the hands together in front of the chest, emphasizing horizontal adduction to work the pectorals. Maintain a slight elbow bend and controlled tempo throughout the movement.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Cable Rope Crossover Seated Row
exercises.notes: Set the low pulley and load an appropriate weight; sit on the bench or seat facing the machine with feet braced on the footplate and knees slightly bent.
instructions: Set the low pulley and load an appropriate weight; sit on the bench or seat facing the machine with feet braced on the footplate and knees slightly bent.
Reach forward and grasp the rope with both hands using a neutral grip (palms facing each other) while keeping the spine neutral and chest open; start with arms fully extended.
Lean the torso slightly forward from the hips (not rounding the lower back) to establish a stable starting position.

Exhale and pull the rope toward your lower chest/upper abdomen by driving the elbows back and down, leading with the elbows and squeezing the shoulder blades together at the end of the concentric phase.
At the peak contraction, optionally separate the rope ends slightly to increase rear delt and upper-back engagement, hold for 1–2 seconds while maintaining a braced core.
Inhale and slowly extend the arms back to the start position under control, maintaining scapular control and a neutral spine throughout.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions and avoid jerking the torso or using momentum; reduce weight if form breaks down.
exercises.description: A seated cable row performed with a rope attachment that emphasizes scapular retraction and the upper back while also engaging the lats and posterior delts. The lifter sits facing the low pulley and pulls the rope toward the torso, squeezing the shoulder blades together.

exercises.name: Cable Seated Row with V-bar
exercises.notes: Attach the V-bar to the low pulley and grasp it with a neutral grip (palms facing each other).
instructions: Sit on the bench facing the cable machine with feet braced against the footplate and knees slightly bent.
Attach the V-bar to the low pulley and grasp it with a neutral grip (palms facing each other). Sit upright with a neutral spine and chest lifted.
Start with your arms extended and shoulders relaxed forward — feel a slight stretch in the lats.
Exhale and initiate the movement by driving your elbows straight back, keeping them close to your sides.
Retract and squeeze the shoulder blades together and pull the V-bar to the lower chest/upper abdomen; avoid excessive torso lean or using momentum.
Hold and squeeze the contracted position for 1 second, focusing on the lats and upper back.
Inhale and slowly extend your arms forward under control until the lats are stretched and you return to the start position.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, maintaining a neutral spine and controlled tempo throughout.
exercises.description: Seated cable row with a V-bar is a horizontal pulling exercise that emphasizes the lats and middle back by pulling the handle toward the torso while seated. It uses a neutral grip to allow a strong elbow-driven pull and a full contraction of the back muscles.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Shrug
exercises.notes: Stand upright (feet about hip-width), hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip at your sides, knees softly bent and core braced.
instructions: Stand upright (feet about hip-width), hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip at your sides, knees softly bent and core braced.
Keep your arms straight (no shrugging with the elbows) and maintain a neutral spine and head position throughout.
Exhale and lift (shrug) both shoulders straight up toward your ears as high as you can, focusing on contracting the top of the traps.
Pause and squeeze at the top for 1–2 seconds, avoiding rolling or rotating the shoulders.
Inhale and slowly lower the shoulders back down to the starting position with control until arms are fully relaxed at your sides.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, keeping movement vertical and controlled; increase weight only if you can maintain form.
exercises.description: Standing exercise using dumbbells to raise the shoulders toward the ears, primarily working the trapezius muscles to develop upper-back and neck strength. Performed with a short vertical shrugging motion while keeping the arms straight.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Incline Row
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and place a loaded barbell within reach on the floor or on low supports.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and place a loaded barbell within reach on the floor or on low supports.
Lie face down (prone) on the bench so your chest is supported, feet braced on the floor or bench for stability, and your torso is fixed against the pad.
Reach down and grasp the barbell with an overhand (pronated) grip about shoulder-width apart. Allow your arms to hang fully extended.
Before pulling, draw your shoulder blades down and together (scapular retraction) to start the movement from the back rather than the arms.
Pull the barbell upward in a controlled path toward your lower chest/upper abdomen by driving your elbows back and slightly out.
At the top, squeeze your shoulder blades together and hold briefly (about 1 second) while keeping the torso pressed to the bench and neck neutral.
Lower the barbell slowly back to full arm extension under control, maintaining scapular tension and avoiding any bouncing or trunk rotation.
Breathe out during the pull and inhale while lowering. Keep movement strict—do not jerk the weight or lift your chest off the bench.
exercises.description: Prone row performed on an incline bench where the torso is supported and a barbell is pulled toward the lower chest/upper abdomen. It emphasizes the latissimus dorsi while also engaging the upper back, rear delts and arm muscles.

exercises.name: Cable Straight Arm Pulldown
exercises.notes: Set a high pulley with a straight bar or rope attachment and select a light-to-moderate weight that allows strict form.
instructions: Set a high pulley with a straight bar or rope attachment and select a light-to-moderate weight that allows strict form.

Stand facing the machine about 1–2 feet away, feet shoulder-width apart and knees slightly bent; hinge forward at the hips so your torso is angled forward (standing bent-over position) with a neutral spine.
Reach up and grasp the bar with an overhand grip (palms facing down) about shoulder-width apart; maintain a small, natural bend in the elbows throughout (do not lock).
Brace your core and depress your shoulder blades slightly (avoid shrugging) so tension is felt in the lats before starting the pull.

Exhale and pull the bar down in a controlled arc by extending the shoulders and pulling with the lats until the bar reaches your upper thighs or mid-thigh level; keep the arms long and the elbows fixed in their slight bend.
Squeeze the lats and hold the bottom position briefly while keeping the ribs down and spine neutral.
Inhale and slowly allow the bar to rise back to the start position under control, resisting the cable so the lats remain under tension; do not let the lower back round as you return.
Repeat for the desired reps, maintaining the hip hinge, neutral spine, and long-arm position on every repetition.
exercises.description: Standing cable straight-arm pulldowns hinge from the hips and pull a high cable down with mostly straight arms to load the lats through a long-range extension. The movement emphasizes scapular depression and shoulder extension while keeping the torso braced.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Row
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat and chest pad (if present) so your feet are flat on the platform and the handles are within comfortable reach with your arms extended.
instructions: Adjust the seat and chest pad (if present) so your feet are flat on the platform and the handles are within comfortable reach with your arms extended.
Select an appropriate weight on the lever stack.
Sit upright with your chest against the pad (or seated tall if no pad), knees slightly bent, feet stable, and maintain a neutral spine and head alignment.
Reach forward and grasp the handles with a firm, neutral grip; start with your arms fully extended and shoulders relaxed.
Initiate the pull by driving your elbows backward and slightly down, retracting your shoulder blades to bring the handles toward your lower chest/upper abdomen.
Keep elbows close to your sides, avoid excessive torso lean or shrugging the shoulders, and breathe out as you pull.
Pause briefly at the peak contraction and actively squeeze your lats and upper back.
Slowly extend your arms to return the handles to the start position under control, allowing the shoulder blades to protract slightly but maintain tension.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining neutral spine and controlled tempo throughout.
exercises.description: Seated lever rows are a horizontal pulling exercise performed on a lever machine that primarily targets the lats while also engaging the middle and upper back and arm muscles. The movement involves pulling handles toward the torso with a scapular retraction and controlled return.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Palm Rotational Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Keep a neutral spine and neck (eyes toward the floor a few feet in front of you).
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart holding a dumbbell in each hand. Slightly bend the knees and hinge forward at the hips until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (bent-over position).
Keep a neutral spine and neck (eyes toward the floor a few feet in front of you). Let the dumbbells hang at arm's length beneath your shoulders with a pronated or neutral grip.
Brace your core and initiate the movement by pulling your shoulder blades back and down (scapular retraction).

Pull the dumbbells upward toward your lower ribs/waist by driving the elbows up and back. As you ascend, rotate your wrists so the palms turn (for example from pronation/neutral toward supination) at the top of the row.
Squeeze the upper back and rear delts at the top for a brief pause, keeping elbows close to the body and avoiding excessive torso rotation or momentum.
Lower the dumbbells slowly back to the starting position while rotating the palms back to the initial orientation, maintaining control throughout.
Repeat for the desired reps. Maintain the hip hinge, slight knee bend, and neutral spine for the entire set.
exercises.description: A bent-over dumbbell row variation where the wrists/palms rotate during the pull to increase scapular retraction and arm involvement. Performed in a hip-hinged position, it emphasizes the upper back while also engaging the lats, rear delts and arms.

exercises.name: Cable Shrug
exercises.notes: Stand facing the cable machine with a handle attached to a low or mid pulley; feet shoulder-width apart and weight evenly distributed.
instructions: Stand facing the cable machine with a handle attached to a low or mid pulley; feet shoulder-width apart and weight evenly distributed.
Grasp one or two handles with a neutral (palms facing the body) or overhand grip and let your arms hang fully extended at your sides; keep a neutral neck and spine.
Slightly bend the knees and brace your core to stabilize the torso; do not lean forward or backward.
Exhale and elevate (shrug) your shoulders straight up toward your ears, focusing on contracting the trapezius—do not roll or rotate the shoulders.
Hold the peak contraction briefly (about 1 second) while maintaining straight arms and a neutral neck.
Inhale and slowly lower the shoulders back down to the start in a controlled manner until traps are fully stretched.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping movement strict and avoiding momentum; reduce weight if you must use arm or torso movement to lift.
exercises.description: Standing cable shrugs target the trapezius by elevating the shoulders against resistance from a cable. Performed with arms straight at the sides, the movement emphasizes a vertical shrug with controlled lowering.

exercises.name: Lever Cross Lat Pulldown
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat and knee pads so your feet are flat on the floor and your thighs are secured under the rollers while seated upright.
instructions: Adjust the seat and knee pads so your feet are flat on the floor and your thighs are secured under the rollers while seated upright.
Select an appropriate weight on the lever stack.
Reach up and grasp the overhead handles with a comfortable grip (neutral or slightly wider than shoulder width), arms fully extended but not locked.
Begin the movement by retracting your shoulder blades, then pull the handles down and slightly across your body toward the upper chest or outer chest, leading with the elbows.
Keep a slight lean back in the torso (not rounded) and avoid using momentum—drive the movement with the lats and upper back, squeezing at the bottom for 1–2 seconds.
Slowly return the handles to the start position with control until your arms are nearly extended and shoulders are not shrugged.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining steady breathing and avoiding excessive swinging or lower-back drive.
exercises.description: Seated lever pulldown performed on a machine where you pull an overhead lever/handle down and slightly across the body to drive shoulder extension and adduction, emphasizing the lats. Focus on scapular retraction and controlled tempo to maximize lat engagement.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Lever Neutral Grip Seated Row (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Load the machine to the desired weight and sit facing the machine with your chest supported on the chest pad and feet braced on the foot platform; knees should be bent and stable.
instructions: Load the machine to the desired weight and sit facing the machine with your chest supported on the chest pad and feet braced on the foot platform; knees should be bent and stable.
Reach forward and grasp the neutral (palms-facing) handles with a firm grip, arms extended and shoulders relaxed; start with a tall, braced torso and a tight core.
Exhale and pull the handles straight back by driving your elbows toward your hips, keeping elbows close to your body and squeezing the shoulder blades together at peak contraction.
Hold the contracted position for about 1 second while maintaining a stable chest on the pad and avoiding excessive torso lean or momentum.
Inhale and slowly extend your arms under control, allowing the shoulder blades to protract and return to the starting position without dropping the weight.
Repeat for desired repetitions, maintaining controlled tempo and full range of motion on each rep.
exercises.description: Seated neutral-grip lever row performed on a plate-loaded machine to target the latissimus dorsi while stabilizing the torso against a pad. Pull the handles toward the torso with a neutral (palms-facing) grip, squeeze the shoulder blades, then slowly return to the start.

exercises.name: Lever Pronated Grip Seated Row (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Reach forward and grasp the handles with a pronated (overhand) grip so your palms face down and arms are fully extended.
instructions: Sit on the machine seat with knees bent and feet planted on the footrests; brace your chest against the chest pad (if present) and sit upright.
Reach forward and grasp the handles with a pronated (overhand) grip so your palms face down and arms are fully extended.
Set your spine neutral, retract your shoulder blades slightly, and maintain a tall chest—do not round your back.
Initiate the movement by driving your elbows straight back, pulling the handles toward your lower chest/upper abdomen while squeezing the shoulder blades together.
Pause briefly at the peak contraction and feel the lats and mid-back engage; avoid excessive torso lean or using momentum.
Slowly extend your arms back to the start position in a controlled manner until your arms are nearly straight and the lats are stretched.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, keeping consistent tempo, full range of motion, and a firm grip throughout.
exercises.description: Seated pronated-grip lever row performed on a plate-loaded machine to develop the lats and mid-upper back by pulling a lever toward the torso using an overhand grip. Emphasizes scapular retraction and elbow drive while maintaining an upright, braced seated position.

exercises.name: Cable Straight Arm Pulldown
exercises.notes: Attach a straight bar or rope to a high pulley and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Attach a straight bar or rope to a high pulley and select an appropriate weight.
Stand facing the machine with feet shoulder-width apart, hinge at the hips and lean forward slightly so your torso is at about a 30–45° angle (standing, hip-hinged position).
Grab the bar with an overhand grip hands shoulder-width apart and extend your arms fully in front of you so the cable is taut; maintain a small, natural bend in the elbows (do not hyperextend).
Brace your core and pull your shoulder blades down and back to engage the lats before initiating the movement.

Keeping the arms straight (maintaining the slight elbow bend), drive the bar down in an arc toward the thighs by extending the shoulder (pulling the upper arms down and back) until the bar reaches hip or upper-thigh level.
Pause briefly at the bottom and squeeze the lats and upper back while maintaining a stable, braced core and neutral spine (avoid rounding or excessive lumbar extension).
Slowly return the bar to the start position with control, allowing the shoulders to flex while maintaining tension in the lats; repeat for desired reps.

Common form tips: keep the torso still (avoid using momentum or excessive torso lean), maintain a slight elbow bend, and focus on scapular depression and lat contraction rather than pulling with the forearms.
exercises.description: Standing cable straight-arm pulldowns hinge the torso forward and pull a high-set cable down with mostly straight arms to target the latissimus dorsi. The movement emphasizes scapular depression and shoulder extension while the core stabilizes the body.

exercises.name: Lever High Row (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Sit upright on the machine with feet flat on the floor and chest/torso stabilized against the pad (or chest pad if present).
instructions: Sit upright on the machine with feet flat on the floor and chest/torso stabilized against the pad (or chest pad if present). Adjust seat so handles are at or slightly above shoulder height when arms are extended.
Grasp the handles with a neutral or overhand grip, arms extended and shoulders relaxed. Take a full breath and brace your core.

Initiate the movement by retracting the shoulder blades and pulling the handles down and back toward the upper chest/near clavicle, leading with the elbows and keeping them at roughly shoulder height (elbows flared).
Squeeze the shoulder blades and upper back at the end of the concentric phase for 1 second, keeping the torso still and avoiding excessive trunk lean or momentum.
Slowly extend the arms back to the start position under control (eccentric phase), maintaining tension in the upper back and preventing the weights from slamming.
Repeat for the desired reps. Breathe out during the pull and inhale during the release.

Common form tips: keep the neck neutral, avoid shrugging the shoulders up, don’t use excessive body swing, and adjust seat/handle height so the pull targets the upper back rather than purely the lats.
exercises.description: Seated plate-loaded high row where you pull overhead/forward handles toward the upper chest to emphasize the upper back (rhomboids, mid traps and rear delts) while also engaging lats and arm muscles. The torso is stabilized against a pad and movement is performed in a controlled pull-and-release.

exercises.name: Lever Pronated Grip Seated Scapula Retraction Shrug (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height and load the machine so you can reach the handles with your arms extended and feet flat on the footrests; sit upright with a neutral spine and knees bent.
instructions: Adjust the seat height and load the machine so you can reach the handles with your arms extended and feet flat on the footrests; sit upright with a neutral spine and knees bent.
Grasp the handles with a pronated (overhand) grip and let your shoulders relax down and slightly forward to set the start position.
Initiate the movement by pulling the handles back using your shoulder blades: actively retract (pinch) the scapulae together while keeping the torso stable and chest neutral.
As you retract the scapulae, elevate your shoulders into a controlled shrug (shoulder elevation) and squeeze your traps and upper back at the top for 1–2 seconds.
Slowly reverse the motion: lower the shoulders and allow the scapulae to protract back to the start position under control — do not let the weight slam forward.
Keep the arms relatively straight with a slight bend at the elbows, avoid using momentum or torso swing, and breathe out during the contraction and in during the return.
exercises.description: Seated, pronated-grip plate-loaded exercise that emphasizes scapular retraction and an upward shrug to target the trapezius and upper back. Performed from a seated position using the machine handles to actively pinch the shoulder blades together and elevate the shoulders.

exercises.name: Cable Shrug
exercises.notes: Set a pulley to low or mid position and attach single handles or a straight bar; choose a moderate weight.
instructions: Set a pulley to low or mid position and attach single handles or a straight bar; choose a moderate weight.
Stand upright facing the machine with feet hip-width apart, knees soft, and chest up; grasp the handles with a neutral or overhand grip so your arms hang straight at your sides.
Keep your torso stationary and arms straight (small bend in the elbows is fine); maintain a neutral neck and look forward.
Exhale and lift your shoulders straight up toward your ears by contracting the traps — avoid rolling or rotating the shoulders.
Pause at the top for 1 second, focusing on a strong contraction in the upper traps.
Inhale and lower the shoulders slowly and under control back to the start position until traps are fully stretched.
Repeat for the desired reps, maintaining an upright posture and avoiding momentum to ensure the traps are doing the work.
exercises.description: A standing cable shrug performed with handles at the sides to elevate the shoulders and load the upper trapezius. The movement isolates upward shoulder elevation while keeping the arms nearly straight.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Shrug
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet hip- to shoulder-width apart holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides (neutral grip, palms facing the body).
instructions: Stand upright with feet hip- to shoulder-width apart holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides (neutral grip, palms facing the body).
Keep a neutral spine and neck, chest up, and arms straight but not locked; maintain a slight bend in the knees.
Exhale and lift your shoulders straight upward toward your ears — move only the shoulders, not the arms or torso.
Pause and squeeze the top of the traps for 1–2 seconds while keeping the neck relaxed (do not jut the chin forward).
Inhale and lower the shoulders back down under control to the starting position; avoid letting the weights drop.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining slow, controlled reps and avoiding any rolling or circular shoulder motion.
If grip is limiting, consider using lifting straps; for variation you can perform seated shrugs to reduce lower body involvement.
exercises.description: Standing dumbbell shrugs raise the shoulders straight up toward the ears to primarily target the trapezius and supporting shoulder/neck muscles. Use a controlled motion with a brief squeeze at the top and avoid rolling the shoulders.

exercises.name: Cable Shrug
exercises.notes: Stand facing the cable machine with a handle attached to a low or mid pulley; feet shoulder-width apart and weight evenly distributed.
instructions: Stand facing the cable machine with a handle attached to a low or mid pulley; feet shoulder-width apart and weight evenly distributed.
Grasp one or two handles with a neutral (palms facing the body) or overhand grip and let your arms hang fully extended at your sides; keep a neutral neck and spine.
Slightly bend the knees and brace your core to stabilize the torso; do not lean forward or backward.
Exhale and elevate (shrug) your shoulders straight up toward your ears, focusing on contracting the trapezius—do not roll or rotate the shoulders.
Hold the peak contraction briefly (about 1 second) while maintaining straight arms and a neutral neck.
Inhale and slowly lower the shoulders back down to the start in a controlled manner until traps are fully stretched.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping movement strict and avoiding momentum; reduce weight if you must use arm or torso movement to lift.
exercises.description: Standing cable shrugs target the trapezius by elevating the shoulders against resistance from a cable. Performed with arms straight at the sides, the movement emphasizes a vertical shrug with controlled lowering.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Walking Lunge
exercises.notes: Step 1: Position the barbell across your upper back (high-bar position) and grip it firmly with hands just outside shoulder width; stand tall with feet hip-width apart and core braced (standing).
instructions: Step 1: Position the barbell across your upper back (high-bar position) and grip it firmly with hands just outside shoulder width; stand tall with feet hip-width apart and core braced (standing).

Step 2: Maintain an upright torso and tight abs; take a controlled long step forward with one foot (about 2–3 feet depending on leg length) so your stride allows the front thigh to descend toward parallel.

Step 3: Lower your body by bending both knees until the front thigh is about parallel to the floor and the back knee is close to the ground; keep the front knee tracking over the toes and avoid letting it collapse inward.
Step 4: Push through the front heel to extend the front hip and knee, driving the back leg forward and bringing the rear foot up to pass the front foot (walking motion) into the next step.
Step 5: Continue alternating forward steps for the desired number of reps, keeping an even, controlled tempo and breathing out as you push up.
Step 6: Keep the barbell stable on the upper back, shoulders slightly retracted, and avoid excessive forward lean; if balance becomes difficult, reduce weight or shorten stride.
Step 7: To finish, carefully step back to the rack area if needed or come to a stop, then rerack the barbell with proper form or set it down safely.
exercises.description: A loaded, forward-stepping lunge that emphasizes the front thigh (quadriceps) while also recruiting the glutes and hamstrings for hip extension and balance. The barbell rests across the upper back as you take controlled steps forward and lower into each lunge.

exercises.name: Barbell Full squat (with rack)
exercises.notes: Set the safety pins and rack J-hooks so the bar sits at about mid-chest height when standing; load the bar and secure collars.
instructions: Set the safety pins and rack J-hooks so the bar sits at about mid-chest height when standing; load the bar and secure collars.
Stand under the bar with it resting across your upper back/traps (not on the neck). Grip the bar with hands just outside shoulder width.
Unrack the bar by straightening your legs to lift it off the hooks, then take 1–2 small steps back so the rack is clear; stand tall with feet shoulder-width apart and toes slightly turned out.
Brace your core (tighten abs), keep chest up and eyes forward. Inhale and begin the descent by pushing hips back and bending knees, keeping knees tracking over toes.
Lower to full depth (hips at or below parallel to the knees) while maintaining a neutral spine and weight distributed through the mid-foot and heels.
Drive through the heels and mid-foot, exhale, and extend hips and knees to return to a fully upright standing position while maintaining a tight core and upright torso.
Step forward and carefully re-rack the bar on the hooks. Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
exercises.description: Barbell back squat performed from a rack where the lifter unracks the bar, steps back and squats to full depth (hips at or below parallel) before returning to standing. This compound lower-body movement primarily loads the quadriceps while also engaging the glutes, hamstrings and core for stability.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Calf Raise (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Place the balls of your feet on the edge of the foot platform so your heels can hang freely; grip the machine handles for stability.
instructions: Sit on the machine seat with knees bent (approximately 90°) and your lower thighs positioned under the padded lever so the pad rests comfortably on top of the thighs.
Place the balls of your feet on the edge of the foot platform so your heels can hang freely; grip the machine handles for stability.
Load the lever arm with the desired weight plates and ensure the machine is secured before beginning.
Start with your heels lowered below the platform to get a full stretch in the calves.
Exhale and drive through the balls of your feet to plantarflex the ankles, raising your heels as high as possible by contracting the calf muscles.
Pause and squeeze the calves briefly at the top (1–2 seconds) while maintaining a neutral spine and firm grip on the handles.
Inhale and lower your heels slowly and with control back to the starting stretched position — avoid bouncing.
Repeat for the planned number of repetitions, maintaining a slow, controlled tempo and keeping the knees stationary under the pad throughout the set.
exercises.description: Seated calf raise performed on a plate-loaded lever machine; with knees bent and a padded lever pressing on the thighs, you plantarflex the ankles to raise the heels and lift the load, isolating the calves (mainly the soleus).

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Front Rack Lunge
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet hip-width apart and the barbell racked across the front of your shoulders (front-rack) with elbows high and upper arms roughly parallel to the floor; grip the bar with either a clean grip or cross-arm position.
instructions: Stand upright with feet hip-width apart and the barbell racked across the front of your shoulders (front-rack) with elbows high and upper arms roughly parallel to the floor; grip the bar with either a clean grip or cross-arm position.
Brace your core, keep your chest tall and shoulders back to maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Take a controlled long step forward with one foot (standing position to forward lunge), landing on the heel of the front foot.

Lower your body by bending both knees until the front thigh is approximately parallel to the floor and the back knee is close to the ground (about 90° at the front knee); keep the front knee tracking over the toes and avoid letting it collapse inward.
Pause briefly at the bottom while maintaining an upright torso and tight core; keep the bar stable on the front shoulders by keeping elbows up.
Drive through the front heel to push yourself back to the starting standing position, fully extending the front hip and knee.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, then switch legs and perform the same number of repetitions on the opposite side.
Safety notes: choose a weight that allows you to keep an upright torso and high elbows; if balance is difficult, reduce the load or perform stationary split squats until comfortable.
exercises.description: A loaded lunge performed from a front-rack barbell position that primarily targets the quadriceps while also engaging the glutes, hamstrings and core for stability. The bar is held on the front shoulders with elbows high while stepping forward and lowering into a controlled lunge.

exercises.name: Barbell Squat
exercises.notes: Set the bar in a squat rack at about mid-chest height.
instructions: Set the bar in a squat rack at about mid-chest height. Position yourself under the bar so it rests across your upper traps (high-bar) or rear delts (low-bar) depending on preference.
Grip the bar with hands just outside shoulder width, elbows pointing down and chest up. Stand with feet roughly shoulder-width apart and toes pointed slightly outward.
Brace your core and take a full breath in, then unrack the bar by extending your legs and taking 1–2 controlled steps backward to clear the rack; establish a stable, shoulder-width stance.
Keep a neutral spine and an upright chest. Initiate the movement by pushing your hips back slightly and bending at the knees, sending your knees outward to track over your toes.
Descend in a controlled manner until your thighs are at least parallel to the floor (or deeper if mobility allows) while keeping weight on your midfoot/heels and maintaining a braced core.
Avoid knee collapse (valgus) — consciously push knees outward and keep toes engaged. Do not let the lower back round; maintain a neutral spine throughout.
Drive through your heels and midfoot to stand back up, exhaling as you ascend and extending hips and knees simultaneously until fully upright.
Reset your breathing and bracing between reps as needed. When finished, step forward under control and re-rack the bar securely.
Use appropriate weight, a spotter or safety pins in the rack if lifting near maximum loads, and adjust stance or bar position to reduce discomfort in shoulders or lower back.
exercises.description: Barbell back squat is a compound, standing lower-body exercise that primarily develops the quadriceps while also engaging the glutes, hamstrings and core for stability. The barbell rests across the upper back while you descend by bending hips and knees, then drive back up to standing.

exercises.name: Barbell Mixed grip Deadlift
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips and bend the knees to lower your torso until you can grip the bar; use a mixed grip (one palm facing you, one palm facing away) with hands slightly outside the knees.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart with the bar over the mid-foot while standing (upright position to start).
Hinge at the hips and bend the knees to lower your torso until you can grip the bar; use a mixed grip (one palm facing you, one palm facing away) with hands slightly outside the knees.
Set your back: pull the shoulder blades back, lift the chest, and maintain a neutral spine (avoid rounding) while your hips sit between a low and mid position.
Brace your core and take a full breath, keep the bar close to your shins (bar touches or tracks just above the mid-foot).
Drive through the heels and extend the hips and knees simultaneously to lift the bar in a controlled hip-hinge, keeping the bar close to your body and shoulders over or slightly in front of the bar.
Stand tall at the top by fully extending the hips and squeezing the glutes; avoid excessive lumbar extension (no hyperextension of the spine).
Lower the bar under control by hinging at the hips first, then bending the knees when the bar passes them, returning the bar to the floor while maintaining a neutral spine (repeat for desired reps).
If using heavy loads, alternate which hand is supinated between sets to balance grip stress and reduce muscle imbalances.
exercises.description: A conventional barbell deadlift using a mixed (over/under) grip to improve grip security while lifting; it trains the hip hinge to drive the bar from the floor to standing, emphasizing the posterior chain. The mixed grip helps prevent the bar from rolling in heavy lifts.

exercises.name: Lever Lying Leg Curl
exercises.notes: Adjust the machine so the hip support pad allows your hips to stay flat on the bench and the lever pivot aligns with your knees; select appropriate weight.
instructions: Adjust the machine so the hip support pad allows your hips to stay flat on the bench and the lever pivot aligns with your knees; select appropriate weight.
Lie prone (face down) on the bench with your knees just off the edge, chest supported, and hook your lower calves/ankles under the padded lever.
Grip the handles or edge of the bench for stability and keep your hips pressed into the bench throughout the movement.
Exhale and curl your heels up toward your glutes by flexing the knees until you reach a full but comfortable contraction.
Pause briefly at the top and squeeze the hamstrings and glutes.
Inhale and slowly lower the lever back to the start with controlled extension of the knees, avoiding letting the hips rise.
Perform repetitions with steady tempo, focusing on full range of motion and control rather than momentum.
exercises.description: Prone (face down) leg curl performed on a lever machine that isolates the hamstrings by bending the knees to lift a pad toward the glutes. The movement emphasizes knee flexion with a controlled eccentric return.

exercises.name: Lever Leg Extension
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat so your back is fully supported and the knee joint aligns with the machine's pivot; place the lower shin pad just above the ankles.
instructions: Adjust the seat so your back is fully supported and the knee joint aligns with the machine's pivot; place the lower shin pad just above the ankles.
Sit upright with feet under the roller pad, grip the side handles or seat edges for stability, and keep your chest lifted and core engaged.
Start with knees bent to about 90 degrees (or the machine's starting position) and inhale to prepare.
Exhale and extend your legs by straightening the knees until they are almost fully extended — do not lock the knees at the top.
Pause briefly (0.5–1 second) at the top to feel the contraction in the quads.
Inhale and slowly lower the lever back to the starting position with a controlled eccentric motion until knees return to the initial bend.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions while keeping the torso still and avoiding momentum or bouncing.
exercises.description: Seated leg extension performed on a lever machine that isolates the quadriceps by extending the knees against a padded lever. The exercise focuses on controlled knee extension and eccentric lowering to target the front of the thighs.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Calf Raise (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Place the balls of your feet on the edge of the foot platform with heels hanging freely off the platform.
instructions: Sit on the seated calf raise machine so your knees are bent at about 90 degrees and your back is upright (seated position).
Place the balls of your feet on the edge of the foot platform with heels hanging freely off the platform.
Position the padded lever across your thighs just above the knees and load the desired plates on the lever (plate-loaded).
Grasp the handles or machine grips for stability and brace your core.
Begin with your heels lowered until you feel a stretch in the calves (ankle dorsiflexion).
Drive through the balls of your feet to raise your heels as high as possible (ankle plantarflexion), lifting the lever and plates.
Pause and squeeze your calves at the top for 1–2 seconds while keeping knees bent and torso stable.
Lower your heels slowly and under control back to the starting stretched position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, avoiding bouncing or using hip/knee extension to assist the movement.
exercises.description: Seated, plate-loaded calf raise that isolates the calf musculature by plantarflexing the ankles while the knees are bent; the lever pad rests on the thighs and weight is applied via a plate-loaded lever. Performed from a seated position to emphasize the soleus and overall calf development.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Front Squat
exercises.notes: Set the barbell in a rack at about shoulder height.
instructions: Set the barbell in a rack at about shoulder height. Step under the bar and position it across the front of your shoulders (front of delts/upper chest) using either a clean (front-rack) grip or a cross-arm hold.
Stand up to unrack the bar, take one or two small steps back, and place your feet about shoulder-width apart with toes pointed slightly out.
Raise your elbows so your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor and brace your abs to keep the torso upright throughout the movement.
Inhale, push your hips back slightly and bend at the knees to descend into a squat; keep your chest up, elbows high, and knees tracking over your toes.
Continue lowering until your thighs reach at least parallel to the floor (or slightly below if mobility allows) while maintaining a neutral spine.
Exhale and drive through your heels and midfoot to extend your knees and hips, returning to the standing start position with control.
Maintain tight abs and high elbows on every rep; avoid letting the knees cave inward or the torso fold forward. Rack the bar safely when finished.
exercises.description: A front-loaded barbell squat where the barbell is held across the front of the shoulders, emphasizing upright torso and knee extension to target the quadriceps. Performed by descending into a squat and driving back up while keeping elbows high and core braced.

exercises.name: Barbell Low Bar Squat with Rack
exercises.notes: Set the rack height so the bar sits at about mid-chest when standing; load the bar and secure collars.
instructions: Set the rack height so the bar sits at about mid-chest when standing; load the bar and secure collars.
Step under the bar and position it low across the rear shoulders / lower trapezius (just below the top of the posterior delts), hands gripping the bar slightly wider than shoulder-width.
Unrack the bar by straightening the legs, then take 1–2 small steps back to clear the rack and plant your feet shoulder-width to slightly wider with toes turned out about 10–30 degrees.
Brace your core and maintain a neutral spine and upright chest with eyes forward; squeeze the shoulder blades and keep the elbows slightly down and back to create a solid shelf for the bar.
Initiate the descent by pushing the hips back and bending the knees simultaneously, keeping knees tracking over the toes and weight distributed through the midfoot and heels.
Lower until the thighs are at least parallel to the floor (or to your comfortable depth while keeping a neutral spine), maintaining the low-bar placement and tight torso throughout.
Drive upward by pressing through the heels/midfoot, extending the hips and knees together while keeping the chest up and core braced.
When standing tall at the top, stabilize the bar, step forward to the rack, and carefully re-rack the bar onto the rack supports.
exercises.description: A compound lower-body squat performed with the barbell set low across the rear deltoids/traps using a rack; it develops thigh strength while recruiting the posterior chain and core for stability. The low-bar position shifts some load onto the hips and upper back compared with a high-bar squat.

exercises.name: Barbell one leg hip thrust
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench and load a barbell with an appropriate weight; optionally use a pad on the bar for comfort.
instructions: Set up a flat bench and load a barbell with an appropriate weight; optionally use a pad on the bar for comfort.
Sit on the floor with your upper back (around the shoulder blade area) against the bench edge and the barbell positioned over your hips.

Roll the barbell onto your hips while leaning back so your shoulder blades remain supported on the bench; bend both knees and plant one foot flat on the floor while extending the other leg straight or slightly bent and held off the floor.
Grip the barbell on either side of your hips to stabilize it and brace your core (tighten your abs) and keep your chin slightly tucked.

Drive through the heel of the planted foot, extend the hip of the working leg, and lift your hips upward until your hips are in line with your torso and knee (forming a straight line from shoulders to knee).
Squeeze the glutes hard at the top for 1–2 seconds while keeping the non-working leg off the floor and hips level (avoid rotating or dropping a hip).
Slowly lower your hips under control until your glutes are just above the floor (or until you feel a full stretch), then repeat for the desired reps before switching legs.
Keep movements smooth and controlled, maintain core tension to protect the spine, and use a lighter weight if you cannot keep hips level or control the descent.
exercises.description: A single-leg variation of the barbell hip thrust performed with the upper back resting on a bench; the working leg drives the hips upward against the barbell to strongly activate the glutes. The non-working leg is kept off the floor to increase unilateral load and demand on hip extensors and core stabilization.

exercises.name: Smith Standing Leg Calf Raise
exercises.notes: Set the Smith bar to a comfortable shoulder height and place a stable raised platform or block under your feet so your heels can drop below the platform.
instructions: Set the Smith bar to a comfortable shoulder height and place a stable raised platform or block under your feet so your heels can drop below the platform.

Step under the bar and position it across your upper back/trapezius (or hold it at hip level if demonstrated), then unlock the Smith carriage and stand with feet hip-width apart and toes pointing forward on the platform edge.
Keep knees straight or with a very slight bend, chest up, and core engaged for a stable upright posture.
Exhale and press through the balls of both feet to raise your heels as high as possible, fully plantarflexing the ankles and squeezing the calves at the top.
Hold the top position briefly, then inhale and slowly lower your heels down below the platform level to achieve a full stretch in the calves.
Control the movement; avoid bouncing or using trunk momentum. Repeat for the desired repetitions, then carefully re-rack the Smith bar when finished.
exercises.description: A standing calf raise performed on a Smith machine that increases resistance for plantarflexion to develop the gastrocnemius and soleus. The lifter rises onto the balls of the feet from a raised surface then lowers the heels below the platform for a full stretch.

exercises.name: Barbell Snatch Deadlift
exercises.notes: Setup: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width (or slightly wider for snatch-style), toes pointed slightly out, barbell on the floor over mid-foot.
instructions: Setup: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width (or slightly wider for snatch-style), toes pointed slightly out, barbell on the floor over mid-foot. Use a wide snatch grip (hands well outside knees) with a pronated grip.

Hinge and brace: Bend at the hips and knees to reach the bar — hips should be lower than a clean deadlift but higher than a full squat; keep chest up, spine neutral, shoulders slightly in front of the bar and eyes looking forward or slightly down.
Grip and tension: Grip the bar tightly with both hands, pull the slack out of the bar by taking a deep breath and bracing your core, engage lats to keep the bar close to your shins.
First pull: Drive through the heels by extending the knees and hips simultaneously, keeping the torso angle constant; focus on pushing the floor away rather than pulling with the arms.
Bar path and extension: Keep the bar close to the body as you extend the knees and hips to full extension, squeezing the glutes at the top and maintaining a tall chest and straight spine.
Lockout and lower: Do not hyperextend the lower back at lockout; control the descent by pushing the hips back, bending the knees, and lowering the bar back to the floor along the same path.
Breathing and reps: Exhale or hold a controlled breath at the top, reset tension between reps by re-bracing the core and lats before initiating the next pull.
Coaching cues and safety: Keep the bar close, avoid rounding the back, ensure knees track over toes, and use lighter weight when learning the wide snatch grip to protect the lower back and shoulders.
exercises.description: The barbell snatch deadlift is a wide-grip deadlift variation that trains the initial pull position for the snatch, emphasizing knee extension and quad drive while also loading the posterior chain and upper back. It uses a very wide (snatch) grip and a slightly lower hip position than a conventional deadlift.

exercises.name: Cable Romanian Deadlift
exercises.notes: Setup: attach a single handle or straight bar to the low pulley and stand facing the machine with feet hip‑width apart; grab the handle with both hands (overhand) and step back until there is light tension in the cable.
instructions: Setup: attach a single handle or straight bar to the low pulley and stand facing the machine with feet hip‑width apart; grab the handle with both hands (overhand) and step back until there is light tension in the cable.
Start position: stand tall with a slight bend in the knees, shoulders down and back, chest lifted and core braced (standing, not kneeling or prone), arms extended holding the handle in front of you.

Descent (hip hinge): initiate the movement by pushing your hips straight back (not by bending the knees more), keeping a neutral spine and neutral head position; allow your torso to lower toward parallel to the floor until you feel a stretch in the hamstrings or until your torso is roughly parallel.
Maintain arm position: keep your arms long and relaxed (the cable provides the load) and avoid pulling with the arms or rounding the upper or lower back.
Ascent (return): drive the hips forward by contracting the glutes and hamstrings, returning to the upright start position while keeping the core engaged and avoiding hyperextension of the lower back.
Breathing and tempo: inhale on the descent, exhale as you drive the hips forward; control the descent and avoid bouncing at the bottom.

Form cues and safety: keep a slight bend in the knees throughout, maintain a neutral spine (do not round the back), keep the cable aligned with the midline of the body, and reduce weight if you cannot keep proper hip hinge mechanics.
exercises.description: A hip‑hinge variation of the Romanian deadlift using a low cable to load the posterior chain; emphasizes hamstring lengthening under tension while the cable provides constant forward pull. Performed standing, keeping a neutral spine and slight knee bend while driving the hips back and then forward.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Leg Curl
exercises.notes: Sit upright on the seated leg curl machine with your back firmly against the backrest and feet positioned under the lower padded roller just above the heels/ankles.
instructions: Sit upright on the seated leg curl machine with your back firmly against the backrest and feet positioned under the lower padded roller just above the heels/ankles.
Adjust the thigh/pelvic pad so it rests snugly on top of your thighs to prevent your hips from rising during the movement; grip the handles for stability.
Start with knees nearly extended but not fully locked, torso upright and core engaged to stabilize the spine.
Exhale and curl the lower leg by flexing the knees, driving the padded roller down and back toward your glutes in a controlled motion.
Pause and squeeze the hamstrings at the top of the movement without lifting your hips off the seat.
Inhale and slowly return to the start position, fully extending the knees under control without letting the weight slam or locking the knees.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions while keeping the torso still and avoiding swinging or using momentum.
exercises.description: Seated leg curl on a lever machine isolates knee flexion to train the hamstrings, with assistance from the glutes and calves. Performed seated with a padded lever that is pulled by bending the knees and bringing the heels toward the buttocks.

exercises.name: Lever One Leg Extension
exercises.notes: Adjust the machine seat so your back is flat against the backrest and your knee aligns with the machine's pivot point; place the lower shin pad just above the ankle of the working leg.
instructions: Adjust the machine seat so your back is flat against the backrest and your knee aligns with the machine's pivot point; place the lower shin pad just above the ankle of the working leg.
Sit upright with your non-working leg resting relaxed, grip the side handles for stability, and brace your core.
Start with the working knee bent at about 90 degrees and the foot under the pad.
Exhale and extend the working leg by straightening the knee until it is nearly locked out but avoid full hyperextension; pause 1–2 seconds at the top.
Inhale and slowly lower the weight back to the start position with controlled tension, keeping the movement smooth.
Complete the desired reps on one leg, then switch to the other leg; maintain an upright posture and avoid using momentum or swinging the torso.
exercises.description: A single-leg seated lever extension that isolates the quadriceps by extending the knee against resistance on a leg extension machine. Performed one leg at a time to improve quad strength and muscular balance.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.
