exercises.name: Dumbbell Clean
exercises.notes: Stand tall holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides, feet about hip-width apart, knees soft and core braced (standing position).
instructions: Stand tall holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides, feet about hip-width apart, knees soft and core braced (standing position).
Hinge at the hips and push your butt back while maintaining a flat back, allowing a slight bend in the knees so the dumbbells hang at mid-shin (hip-hinge position).

Explosively extend the hips and knees (triple extension) by driving through the feet and forcefully straightening the body; simultaneously shrug the shoulders and pull the dumbbells upward close to the body.

As the dumbbells reach chest height, quickly rotate the elbows forward and under the weights so you 'catch' them against the front of your shoulders in a racked position (partial squat catch is optional).

Absorb the load by slightly bending the knees into a shallow squat if needed, then stand fully upright to complete the rep with the dumbbells resting at the shoulders (upright standing racked position).

Lower the dumbbells back to the hanging position in a controlled manner by reversing the motion: push the hips back, bend the knees, and let the weights return to mid-shin before starting the next rep.
Keep the core engaged, chest up, wrists neutral, and maintain the dumbbells close to the body throughout to protect the lower back and maximize efficiency.
exercises.description: A dynamic compound lift where dumbbells are explosively pulled from a hip-hinge to a racked position at the shoulders, using a quick leg drive and shrug. Trains power production through the hips and legs while also recruiting upper-back and shoulder muscles to receive the load.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Split Squat
exercises.notes: Stand upright in a split stance with one foot forward and the other foot stepped back (rear foot on the toes).
instructions: Stand upright in a split stance with one foot forward and the other foot stepped back (rear foot on the toes). Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides, arms straight and palms facing the body.
Brace your core and keep your torso tall and neutral (avoid excessive forward lean). Position feet so that when you lower, the front knee tracks over the front toes without collapsing inward.

Begin the descent by bending both knees, lowering your hips straight down until the front thigh is roughly parallel to the floor or the front knee reaches about 90 degrees. Allow the rear knee to move toward the floor but not to rest on it.
Keep weight distributed through the front heel and midfoot; maintain even balance and a stable pelvis throughout the movement.
Pause briefly at the bottom while maintaining an upright torso and engaged core.
Drive through the front heel to straighten the front leg and return to the starting upright split stance. Keep the movement controlled; do not lock the knee at the top.
Repeat for desired repetitions, then switch legs and perform the same number on the opposite side.
exercises.description: A single-leg, stationary lunge performed with dumbbells to load the front leg; it emphasizes the quadriceps while also engaging the glutes and hamstrings for hip stability. The movement is done in a split stance with a controlled descent and drive through the front heel to return to standing.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Calf Raise
exercises.notes: Stand on the edge of a step or aerobic platform with the balls of both feet on the edge and heels hanging off; feet about hip-width apart.
instructions: Stand on the edge of a step or aerobic platform with the balls of both feet on the edge and heels hanging off; feet about hip-width apart.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral grip, arms relaxed; optionally lightly touch a wall or railing for balance (wall-assisted if needed).
Keep the torso upright, chest lifted and gaze forward; maintain a slight bend in the knees (do not lock them).
Inhale, then exhale and contract your calves to raise your heels as high as possible until you are standing on your toes; keep weight evenly distributed across the forefoot.
Pause at the top for 1–2 seconds, focusing on a full contraction of the calves while maintaining core tension.
Inhale and slowly lower your heels below the level of the step until you feel a controlled stretch in the calves.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, controlling both the concentric (raise) and eccentric (lower) phases and breathing rhythmically.
exercises.description: A weighted standing calf raise performed on the edge of a step to load the calf muscles through a full plantarflexion and stretch. Dumbbells add resistance and grip demand while the core and glutes stabilize the body.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Hang Clean
exercises.notes: Hinge slightly at the hips and bend the knees to set a strong athletic posture: neutral spine, chest up, eyes forward and core braced.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and the barbell held at thigh/upper-thigh level (hang position) using a slightly wider-than-shoulder grip, wrists straight and elbows pointed down.
Hinge slightly at the hips and bend the knees to set a strong athletic posture: neutral spine, chest up, eyes forward and core braced.
Initiate the move by forcefully extending the hips and knees (triple extension) and pushing through the midfoot and forefoot to generate upward momentum on the bar.
As the bar rises, shrug the shoulders aggressively and pull the elbows high and out to accelerate the bar close to the body; keep the bar path vertical and close.

Explosively pull yourself under the bar by quickly bending the knees and rotating the elbows under so the bar is received on the front of the shoulders (front-rack) with the elbows high and upper arms roughly parallel to the floor.

Catch the bar in a partial squat or quarter squat (depending on load and intent) with an upright torso, controlled breath, and firm grip; ensure wrist and elbow position supports the bar on the shoulders.
Stand up fully from the catch by extending hips and knees to finish the repetition with hips locked out and shoulders stacked over hips.
Lower the bar in a controlled manner back to the hang position (upper-thigh), reset posture and breathing, then repeat for the desired reps.
Use lighter loads to practice timing and technique before increasing weight; keep the spine neutral throughout and avoid pulling with the arms only.
exercises.description: The barbell hang clean is a power, compound lift performed from a standing hip/upper-thigh position that uses an explosive hip and knee extension to drive the bar upward, followed by pulling yourself under the bar and catching it in a front-rack position. It trains lower-body power, posterior chain drive, upper-back shrugging and a quick catch in the front of the shoulders.

exercises.name: Lever Alternate Leg Press (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Load the machine appropriately and lock the sled/lever into the start position; grasp the side handles for stability.
instructions: Sit on the lever leg press with your back fully supported and feet placed hip-width on the footplate; adjust the seat so knees start at about a 90-degree angle.
Load the machine appropriately and lock the sled/lever into the start position; grasp the side handles for stability.
Place one foot flat and centered on the footplate (working leg) and keep the other foot off the plate or lightly rested without pushing.
Unlock the sled and press through the heel and mid-foot of the working leg, extending the knee until the leg is almost straight — do not fully lock the knee.
Pause briefly at the top with control, then inhale and slowly lower the sled by bending the knee until you return to the starting ~90-degree knee angle.
Keep your lower back pressed into the pad, avoid rotating the hips, and maintain a steady tempo; exhale on the concentric (press) and inhale on the eccentric (return).
Complete the prescribed reps on one leg, re-lock the sled if needed, then switch and perform the same number of reps with the opposite leg.
exercises.description: Seated single-leg (alternate) press on a plate-loaded lever machine that emphasizes knee extension to target the quadriceps while the glutes and hamstrings assist. Performed one leg at a time with the back supported against the seat.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Squat Calf Raise on Leg Press Machine
exercises.notes: Set the lever/leg-press machine to a comfortable weight and sit with your back pressed into the seat (seated/squat position).
instructions: Set the lever/leg-press machine to a comfortable weight and sit with your back pressed into the seat (seated/squat position). Grip the side handles for stability.
Place the balls of your feet on the lower edge of the footplate, feet about hip-width and toes pointing forward; knees should be bent (approximately 90°) throughout the exercise.
Unlock the machine safety if applicable and allow your heels to lower until you feel a stretch in the calves — this is the start position.
Exhale and contract the calves to raise your heels by pressing through the balls of your feet, lifting as high as possible without extending or locking the knees; pause 1 second at the top.
Inhale and slowly lower your heels back toward the start position under control until you feel a stretch in the calves; avoid bouncing.
Maintain a stable torso and fixed knee angle throughout the set so movement comes primarily from the ankle joint.
After completing the set, lock or re-engage the machine safety before releasing the handles and exiting the seat.
exercises.description: A seated, squat-style calf raise performed on a leg-press/lever machine where the user plantarflexes the ankles against the footplate to target the calf muscles while the hips and knees remain in a flexed (seated/squat) position.

exercises.name: Barbell Romanian Deadlift
exercises.notes: Stand upright holding a barbell with an overhand (or mixed) grip, feet about hip-width apart and the bar resting against your thighs; maintain a neutral spine and engaged core.
instructions: Stand upright holding a barbell with an overhand (or mixed) grip, feet about hip-width apart and the bar resting against your thighs; maintain a neutral spine and engaged core.
Set a small, consistent bend in the knees (soft knees) — do not bend into a squat; this is a hinge at the hips, not a knee-dominant lift.
Take a breath in, brace your core, then push your hips back while keeping the chest up and spine neutral; allow the bar to travel down close to the front of your legs.
Lower the bar until you feel a strong but safe stretch in the hamstrings (typically to mid‑shin), keeping the bar close to the body and shoulders pulled back — avoid rounding the lower back.
Pause briefly at the bottom while maintaining tension in the hamstrings and back; keep your head in a neutral alignment (looking a few feet in front of you).
Exhale and drive your hips forward by contracting the glutes and hamstrings to return to the standing start position; do not hyperextend the lower back at the top.
Control the descent and ascent on every rep, using a weight that lets you maintain a neutral spine and full hip hinge pattern.
If grip is limiting, switch to a mixed grip or use straps; always prioritize form over load and stop if you feel lumbar rounding or sharp pain.
exercises.description: A bilateral hip-hinge performed from standing that emphasizes the hamstrings by lowering a loaded barbell along the thighs with a neutral spine and slight knee bend; returned to standing by driving the hips forward and squeezing the glutes. Builds posterior chain strength and improves hip extension.

exercises.name: Barbell Hip Thrust
exercises.notes: Plant your feet flat on the floor hip-width apart with knees bent so shins are near vertical; grip the barbell at the sides to steady it.
instructions: Sit on the floor with your upper back (shoulder blades) against a flat bench and roll the barbell over your legs so it rests in the hip crease; use padding on the bar for comfort.
Plant your feet flat on the floor hip-width apart with knees bent so shins are near vertical; grip the barbell at the sides to steady it.
Brace your core, press through your heels and drive your hips upward until your torso and thighs form a straight line; actively squeeze the glutes at the top.
Hold the top briefly while maintaining a neutral spine and avoiding lumbar hyperextension (keep ribs down and chin slightly tucked).
Lower your hips under control until they are just above the floor (or until a full comfortable range) and immediately begin the next rep.
Adjust foot placement as needed (closer to mid-foot emphasizes glutes, higher placement can increase hamstring involvement) and use a spotter when using heavy loads.
exercises.description: A bench-supported hip extension where a loaded barbell is driven by the hips to target glute strength and hypertrophy while the upper back rests on a bench. The movement emphasizes powerful horizontal hip extension with controlled lowering.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Leg Curl
exercises.notes: Sit upright on the seated leg curl machine with your back firmly against the backrest and feet positioned under the lower padded roller just above the heels/ankles.
instructions: Sit upright on the seated leg curl machine with your back firmly against the backrest and feet positioned under the lower padded roller just above the heels/ankles.
Adjust the thigh/pelvic pad so it rests snugly on top of your thighs to prevent your hips from rising during the movement; grip the handles for stability.
Start with knees nearly extended but not fully locked, torso upright and core engaged to stabilize the spine.
Exhale and curl the lower leg by flexing the knees, driving the padded roller down and back toward your glutes in a controlled motion.
Pause and squeeze the hamstrings at the top of the movement without lifting your hips off the seat.
Inhale and slowly return to the start position, fully extending the knees under control without letting the weight slam or locking the knees.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions while keeping the torso still and avoiding swinging or using momentum.
exercises.description: Seated leg curl on a lever machine isolates knee flexion to train the hamstrings, with assistance from the glutes and calves. Performed seated with a padded lever that is pulled by bending the knees and bringing the heels toward the buttocks.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Split Squat
exercises.notes: Set the barbell on a squat rack at upper‑chest height and load appropriate weight.
instructions: Set the barbell on a squat rack at upper‑chest height and load appropriate weight. Step under the bar and rest it across your upper back/traps with a secure, pronated grip; unrack the bar and take a small step back.

Position your feet in a split stance: one foot forward (working leg) and the other foot back with toes on the floor and heel elevated slightly; keep a distance so the front knee can bend to about 90 degrees at the bottom.
Brace your core and keep your torso upright and chest lifted throughout the movement; gaze forward to help maintain posture.

Slowly lower your body by bending the front knee and pushing the hips back slightly until the front thigh is around parallel to the floor or you reach a comfortable depth; the rear knee should lower toward the floor but not necessarily touch.
Ensure the front knee tracks in line with your toes (avoid collapsing inward) and keep weight primarily through the front heel and midfoot.
Drive through the front heel to extend the front knee and hip, returning to the starting split stance. Exhale on the drive.
Complete the desired number of reps on the working leg, then carefully step the back foot forward, re‑rack the bar, reset, and repeat with the opposite leg.
exercises.description: A unilateral, loaded lower‑body exercise performed in a staggered (split) stance with a barbell on the upper back that emphasizes the front leg's quadriceps while also working the glutes and hamstrings for hip control and the core for stability.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Romanian Deadlift
exercises.notes: Stand upright holding a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip, feet about hip‑width apart and toes pointing forward.
instructions: Stand upright holding a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip, feet about hip‑width apart and toes pointing forward.
Set a slight bend in the knees (soft knees) and brace your core to stabilize the spine before initiating the movement.

Hinge at the hips by pushing your hips straight back while keeping the spine neutral and chest slightly lifted; allow the dumbbells to slide down the front of your thighs and shins, staying close to the body.
Lower until you feel a strong stretch in the hamstrings or the dumbbells reach mid‑shin (maintain neutral cervical alignment, do not round the lower back).
Reverse the motion by driving the hips forward, squeezing the glutes and hamstrings to return to standing while keeping the knees softly bent and the weights close to the legs.
Maintain controlled tempo (slow on the descent, controlled drive on the ascent), keep the shoulders down away from the ears, and avoid using the lower back to lift.
exercises.description: A standing hip‑hinge exercise performed with dumbbells that emphasizes eccentric loading of the hamstrings while the hips move back and forth; the movement also engages the glutes and core to maintain a neutral spine. Keep a slight knee bend and the weights close to the legs throughout.

exercises.name: Barbell Sumo Romanian Deadlift
exercises.notes: Brace your core, set a neutral spine (no rounding) and slightly bend the knees — this is a standing starting position.
instructions: Stand with a wide (sumo) stance, toes turned slightly outward and feet under the sleeves of the barbell so the bar is over mid-foot; grip the bar with hands roughly shoulder-width apart using an overhand or mixed grip.
Brace your core, set a neutral spine (no rounding) and slightly bend the knees — this is a standing starting position.
Begin the rep by hinging at the hips: push your hips back while keeping the barbell close to your legs, maintaining a long neutral spine and tension in the hamstrings and glutes.
Lower the bar in a controlled manner until you feel a stretch in the hamstrings or the bar reaches about mid-shin (or just below the knees), keeping the neck neutral and shoulders pulled back.
Pause briefly at the bottom while keeping the core engaged, then drive the hips forward to return to standing by contracting the glutes — do not hyperextend the lower back at the top.
Keep the knees slightly bent throughout (not a deep squat), maintain the bar close to the body on ascent and descent, and exhale as you drive the hips forward.
Use an appropriate weight that allows you to maintain a neutral spine and proper hinge mechanics for all repetitions.
exercises.description: A wide-stance (sumo) Romanian deadlift is a hip-hinge movement performed with a barbell that emphasizes the glutes while also loading the hamstrings and inner thighs; the lifter maintains a neutral spine and a slight knee bend as the bar travels close to the legs from hip height to mid-shin.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Split Squat Front Foot Elevanted
exercises.notes: Stand in a split stance with the front foot placed on a low step or platform and the rear foot on the floor with toes touching; hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides (standing, upright position).
instructions: Stand in a split stance with the front foot placed on a low step or platform and the rear foot on the floor with toes touching; hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides (standing, upright position).
Set your feet hip-width apart laterally for balance and keep your torso tall with chest up and core braced (avoid leaning forward).

Begin the descent by bending the front knee and hip, lowering your body straight down until the front thigh is about parallel to the floor or you reach a comfortable 90° knee angle; keep the rear knee soft and lowered toward the floor but not touching.
Ensure the front knee tracks in line with your toes (avoid collapsing inward) and distribute weight through the front heel and midfoot as you lower.
Pause briefly at the bottom with control, then drive through the front heel to extend the knee and hip, returning to the starting upright position.
Repeat for the desired reps, then switch legs so the opposite foot becomes the front elevated foot.
Breathing: inhale as you lower, exhale as you push back up. Use a step height and dumbbell weight that allows controlled movement without losing balance.
exercises.description: A unilateral squat variation performed in a split stance with the front foot elevated on a step while holding dumbbells by your sides; it emphasizes the front leg's knee extension (quads) and challenges balance and hip control. The back foot remains on the toes while you lower and raise your body through the front hip and knee.

exercises.name: Barbell Staggered Stance Deadlift
exercises.notes: Approach the bar and grip it just outside your knees with an overhand or mixed grip; keep arms straight and shoulders over the bar.
instructions: Stand with a barbell over mid-foot and adopt a staggered stance: one foot forward flat on the floor and the other foot slightly back (toes touching or on the ball of the foot).
Approach the bar and grip it just outside your knees with an overhand or mixed grip; keep arms straight and shoulders over the bar.
Bend knees slightly and push hips back into a deep hip-hinge while maintaining a neutral spine and a braced core; keep the bar close to your shins.

Drive through the front foot (heel and midfoot) by extending the hips and knees simultaneously, squeezing the hamstrings and glutes to stand upright; keep the rear leg stable and slightly bent or on the toes for balance.
At the top, finish with hips fully extended but avoid hyperextending the lower back; shoulders should be down and back.
Lower the bar under control by hinging at the hips and allowing the knees to flex slightly, maintaining a neutral spine and keeping the bar close to the legs until it returns to the floor.
Repeat for the desired reps, then switch lead legs to work the other side.
exercises.description: A single-leg biased deadlift performed from a staggered standing position that emphasizes a hip-hinge to load the hamstrings and glutes while challenging unilateral stability. The movement lifts a barbell from the floor by extending the hips and knees while keeping the torso stable.

exercises.name: Smith Seated Calf Raise
exercises.notes: Setup the Smith machine: place a flat bench in the rack so you can sit with feet on a raised block or plate with heels hanging off the edge; set the bar height so it rests across your upper thighs/knees when seated.
instructions: Setup the Smith machine: place a flat bench in the rack so you can sit with feet on a raised block or plate with heels hanging off the edge; set the bar height so it rests across your upper thighs/knees when seated.

Sit on the bench with knees bent about 90 degrees, shins vertical, feet hip-width apart and toes pointing forward on the block so only the balls of the feet contact the platform; place the Smith barpad or a pad across your upper thighs just above the knees and unhook the bar.
Brace your core, keep an upright seated posture (not slouched), and grip the bar lightly for stability; ensure your knees remain bent throughout the movement (seated position).
Lower your heels slowly by dorsiflexing the ankles until you feel a stretch in the calves (heels drop below the level of the balls of the feet), keeping control and no bouncing.
Drive through the balls of the feet and contract your calves to raise the heels as high as possible (plantarflexion), hold a brief peak contraction at the top.
Lower back down under control to the starting stretched position and repeat for the desired number of reps. Re-rack the bar safely when finished.
Notes: use a controlled tempo, avoid using knee extension or hip drive to lift the weight, and adjust foot position (toes in/out) if you want to emphasize different parts of the calf.
exercises.description: Seated calf raise performed in a Smith machine; sitting with knees bent under the bar, you raise and lower the heels to work the calf muscles through full ankle plantarflexion and dorsiflexion. This variation emphasizes the soleus portion of the calves due to the knee-flexed position.

exercises.name: Lever Standing Calf Raise
exercises.notes: Set the desired weight on the lever standing calf raise machine and step onto the foot platform so the balls of your feet are on the edge and your heels can hang freely.
instructions: Set the desired weight on the lever standing calf raise machine and step onto the foot platform so the balls of your feet are on the edge and your heels can hang freely.
Position your shoulders under the padded lever, stand tall and grip the machine handles for stability; feet should be hip-width and toes pointing forward (or slightly turned in/out to vary emphasis).
Begin with a full stretch: lower your heels down until you feel a controlled stretch in the calves (keep torso upright and spine neutral).
Drive through the balls of your feet to raise your heels as high as possible, exhaling and keeping knees straight (a slight knee bend shifts emphasis to the soleus if desired).
Pause 1–2 seconds at the top and squeeze the calves, then slowly lower your heels back to the stretched starting position with control—avoid bouncing.
Repeat for the prescribed number of repetitions, maintaining steady tempo and proper alignment throughout.
exercises.description: A machine-based standing calf raise where the shoulders are braced under a padded lever and the heels drop and rise from a platform to load the calf muscles through a full range of motion. Emphasizes plantarflexion to strengthen and develop the gastrocnemius/soleus complex.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Calf Raise (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Place the balls of your feet on the edge of the foot platform with heels hanging freely off the platform.
instructions: Sit on the seated calf raise machine so your knees are bent at about 90 degrees and your back is upright (seated position).
Place the balls of your feet on the edge of the foot platform with heels hanging freely off the platform.
Position the padded lever across your thighs just above the knees and load the desired plates on the lever (plate-loaded).
Grasp the handles or machine grips for stability and brace your core.
Begin with your heels lowered until you feel a stretch in the calves (ankle dorsiflexion).
Drive through the balls of your feet to raise your heels as high as possible (ankle plantarflexion), lifting the lever and plates.
Pause and squeeze your calves at the top for 1–2 seconds while keeping knees bent and torso stable.
Lower your heels slowly and under control back to the starting stretched position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, avoiding bouncing or using hip/knee extension to assist the movement.
exercises.description: Seated, plate-loaded calf raise that isolates the calf musculature by plantarflexing the ankles while the knees are bent; the lever pad rests on the thighs and weight is applied via a plate-loaded lever. Performed from a seated position to emphasize the soleus and overall calf development.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Seated Overhead Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with feet flat on the floor about hip-width apart and torso vertical (back neutral).
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with feet flat on the floor about hip-width apart and torso vertical (back neutral).
Grip the barbell with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, wrists stacked over elbows and palms facing forward; rest the bar at upper chest/clavicle level.
Brace your core and squeeze your shoulder blades down and slightly back to stabilize the spine; keep head neutral with eyes forward.
Inhale, then press the bar upward in a vertical path by extending your elbows and driving through the delts until arms are nearly locked out overhead.
As the bar passes your forehead, slightly tilt your head back to allow clearance, then return your head to neutral at the top.
Pause briefly at the top with the bar roughly over mid-foot, shoulder blades stable and ribs down to avoid excessive lower-back arching.
Exhale and slowly lower the bar under control back to the starting position at the upper chest/clavicle, keeping elbows slightly in front of the body.
Repeat for the desired reps, maintaining a tight core and steady breathing; avoid bouncing the bar off the chest or flaring the ribs.
exercises.description: Seated barbell overhead press performed from a seated, upright position pressing a barbell from the upper chest/clavicle to full overhead lockout. Primarily targets the shoulder delts while engaging triceps and upper traps for assistance and stabilization.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.
instructions: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.

Lie supine on the bench with your head, upper back and glutes in contact with the bench, feet flat on the floor and a small natural arch in the lower back; retract and depress your shoulder blades to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists should be stacked over the elbows and forearms vertical when the bar is over the chest.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar out over the chest while keeping the scapulae stable; lock the elbows briefly before starting the set.
Inhale and lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-chest/sternum (around nipple level), keeping the elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso to protect the shoulders.
Allow the bar to touch or come very close to the chest without bouncing; maintain tension in the pectorals and keep the feet and glutes braced.
Exhale and press the bar upward explosively while keeping the bar path slightly arced toward the hips until the arms are fully extended but not hyperextended.
Pause briefly at the top with the shoulders stable, then begin the next rep. Maintain consistent form and breathing throughout the set.
Use a spotter or safety pins in the rack for heavy sets, and avoid flaring the elbows excessively or letting the lower back lift off the bench.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a compound pressing movement performed lying supine on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and anterior delts. It involves lowering a loaded barbell to the chest and pressing it back up to full arm extension with controlled technique.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Fly
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and lower back in a neutral position.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and lower back in a neutral position.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral or slightly pronated grip and extend the arms straight above your chest, palms facing each other.
Keep a slight, fixed bend in the elbows throughout the movement (do not lock the elbows).
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc, maintaining the slight elbow bend until you feel a stretch in the chest or your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor.
Keep the shoulder blades stable on the bench (no excessive shrugging) and avoid letting the dumbbells travel behind the plane of your torso.
Pause briefly at the bottom under control, then exhale and bring the dumbbells back up along the same arc, focusing on squeezing the pectorals until the weights meet above the sternum.
Perform each rep with controlled tempo and avoid bouncing or using momentum; select a weight that allows full control and a safe range of motion.
exercises.description: A supine resistance exercise performed with dumbbells that isolates the chest by moving the arms in a wide arc from above the sternum to the sides, producing a deep stretch and concentric squeeze of the pectorals.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.

exercises.name: Barbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an adjustable bench to a 30-45° incline and position it inside a power rack or with a spotter and place the barbell on the rack at a height you can reach while seated.
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 30-45° incline and position it inside a power rack or with a spotter and place the barbell on the rack at a height you can reach while seated.
Sit with your back and head firmly against the bench, feet flat on the floor, and brace your core (tighten abs and glutes) to maintain a stable, slight arch in the lower back.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists stacked over elbows and shoulders pulled down and back.
Unrack the bar by straightening your arms and moving the bar directly above your upper chest/shoulder line; have a spotter if needed.
Inhale, bend the elbows, and lower the bar under control to the upper chest/clavicle area (touch point just below the top of the sternum) keeping elbows at roughly 30–60° from the torso.
Pause briefly at the bottom without bouncing, ensuring the forearms remain vertical and the core stays braced.
Exhale and press the bar back up in a slightly backward arc until the arms are fully extended but not locked aggressively at the elbows.
Repeat for the desired repetitions, then carefully re-rack the bar on the uprights with control or have the spotter assist.
exercises.description: The barbell incline bench press is a compound pressing movement performed on an inclined bench that emphasizes the upper portion of the pectorals while also engaging the anterior delts and triceps. It is done from a supine position on an incline bench, pressing a barbell vertically from chest to full arm extension.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Hammer Press
exercises.notes: Set an adjustable bench to a 30–45° incline and select two dumbbells of appropriate weight.
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 30–45° incline and select two dumbbells of appropriate weight.
Sit on the bench and plant your feet flat on the floor; lie back so your head, upper back and hips are supported (supine on an incline bench).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral (palms facing each other) grip; bring the dumbbells to shoulder level with elbows bent (~90°) and wrists stacked over elbows.
Brace your core, retract your shoulder blades slightly, and keep the elbows under the wrists (avoid excessive flaring).
Exhale and press both dumbbells up and slightly together until your arms are almost extended — do not lock the elbows at the top.
Pause briefly at the top, then inhale and lower the dumbbells in a controlled manner back to shoulder level, keeping the same neutral grip.
Repeat for the desired number of reps while maintaining steady tempo, stable bench contact, and neutral wrist alignment.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline bench, dumbbells are pressed upward with a neutral (hammer) grip to emphasize the upper chest. The movement uses a controlled press and descent to work the pectorals with assistance from the delts and triceps.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and place a pair of dumbbells nearby.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and place a pair of dumbbells nearby.
Sit on the bench with the dumbbells resting on your thighs, feet flat on the floor for stability.
Use your legs to help lift the dumbbells as you lie back so the weights are beside your upper chest; hold them with a neutral or slightly supinated grip.
Retract your shoulder blades and press your upper back into the bench so your chest is proud (maintain a stable supine position on the incline).
Plant your feet and brace your core to avoid excessive arching of the spine.
Press the dumbbells upward and slightly together in a controlled path until your arms are almost fully extended (do not lock the elbows).
Exhale as you press up, pause briefly at the top with control, then inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the start position at the upper chest level.
Keep the elbows at a moderate angle (not flared excessively) and the wrists neutral throughout the movement.
Repeat for desired repetitions and use a spotter for heavy sets or if you cannot safely return the weights to your thighs/stand them up.
exercises.description: The dumbbell incline bench press is performed lying supine on an incline bench and pressing dumbbells upward to emphasize the upper portion of the chest. It builds strength and mass in the upper pectorals while also recruiting the front delts and triceps.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Shoulder Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced to maintain a neutral spine.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced to maintain a neutral spine.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with palms facing forward (or slightly rotated) and elbows bent roughly 90 degrees, wrists stacked over elbows.
Inhale, then exhale as you press both dumbbells upward in a controlled path until your arms are nearly extended overhead; avoid fully locking the elbows.
Pause briefly at the top while keeping the shoulders down and chest lifted (do not shrug excessively).
Inhale as you slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting shoulder-height position under control.
Keep the back pressed against the bench and avoid excessive lower-back arching or leaning; repeat for desired reps.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell shoulder press performed from a back-supported bench to drive the weights overhead, primarily working the shoulder (deltoid) muscles with triceps and upper traps assisting. The movement emphasizes vertical pressing strength and shoulder stability.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.

instructions: Stand tall with feet hip‑width apart, knees soft and core braced (standing position).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder level with palms facing each other (palms‑in), elbows pointed slightly forward and wrists neutral.
Keep the torso upright and ribs down; inhale to prepare.
Exhale and press both dumbbells straight overhead in a controlled path until the arms are nearly extended but elbows are not forcibly locked.
Pause briefly at the top with shoulders engaged and scapulae stable (avoid shrugging excessively).
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the start position at shoulder height with control.
Repeat for the desired number of reps while keeping the core engaged and avoiding arching the lower back.
exercises.description: A standing dumbbell shoulder press performed with a neutral (palms‑facing‑in) grip; primarily targets the delts while engaging the triceps and upper chest for pressing and the traps for scapular support. Performed standing to require core stability and upright posture.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Hammer Grip Shoulder Press

Grasp the handles with a hammer (neutral) grip (palms facing each other), elbows bent and positioned slightly forward of the body.

Inhale and lower the handles slowly back to the starting position at shoulder height, maintaining control throughout the descent.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Pullover
exercises.notes: Set an adjustable bench to a 30–45° incline and sit with a dumbbell placed vertically across your palms (hold one head of the dumbbell with both hands) or hold a single dumbbell by the handle with both hands cupped around one end. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 30–45° incline and sit with a dumbbell placed vertically across your palms (hold one head of the dumbbell with both hands) or hold a single dumbbell by the handle with both hands cupped around one end.

Lie back so your upper back and shoulders are supported on the bench (supine on incline), feet flat on the floor for stability and hips down; keep a slight natural arch in the low back but avoid excessive lumbar extension.
Start with the dumbbell held directly over your chest with arms extended but elbows slightly bent and hands steady; brace your core.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbell in a controlled arc behind your head until you feel a comfortable stretch across the lats/pectorals (do not force range or let shoulders shrug upward).
Pause briefly at the bottom while maintaining a slight bend in the elbows and tension in the shoulders/triceps.
Exhale and pull the dumbbell back up in the same arc using your lats and chest, returning to the start position over the chest; keep movement smooth and avoid using momentum.
Keep the elbows slightly bent throughout, avoid locking out at the top, and maintain shoulder blades down and back to reduce neck/shoulder strain.
Repeat for desired reps. Use a lighter weight if you cannot control the descent or if you feel pain in the shoulders or neck.
exercises.description: A supine incline bench pullover performed with a single dumbbell; the weight is moved in an arcing motion from over the chest to behind the head to stretch and contract the lats and chest. Performed slowly to emphasize the stretch and pulling action of the back and front torso muscles.

exercises.name: Cable Underhand Pulldown
exercises.notes: Set the seat height and thigh pads so your knees are secured under the pad and select an appropriate weight; attach a straight bar to the high pulley. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set the seat height and thigh pads so your knees are secured under the pad and select an appropriate weight; attach a straight bar to the high pulley.
Sit upright on the bench with feet flat, torso tall and slightly braced, then reach up and grasp the bar with an underhand (supinated) grip about shoulder-width apart.
Begin with arms fully extended and scapulae slightly protracted (shoulders relaxed up), inhale, then exhale as you pull the bar down by driving your elbows down and back.
Pull the bar to the upper chest/clavicle area while keeping the torso stable and leaning back only slightly; focus on contracting the lats and squeezing the shoulder blades together.
Pause briefly at the bottom, then slowly return the bar under control to the start position with arms nearly straight and a controlled stretch in the lats.
Repeat for the desired reps, avoiding torso momentum or excessive backward lean; keep wrists neutral and core engaged throughout.
exercises.description: Seated cable underhand pulldown (reverse-grip pulldown) is a vertical pulling exercise that emphasizes the latissimus dorsi while using a supinated grip to increase biceps involvement. Performed seated at a high pulley, the bar is pulled down to the upper chest with elbows driven down and back.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Lying Hammer Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with your feet flat on the floor and knees bent; keep your back neutral and shoulder blades gently retracted. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with your feet flat on the floor and knees bent; keep your back neutral and shoulder blades gently retracted.
Pick up a pair of dumbbells and hold them at chest level with a neutral (palms facing each other / hammer) grip and elbows bent about 45 degrees from your torso.
Brace your core, keep your wrists neutral, and press the dumbbells upward in a controlled path until your arms are nearly fully extended but not locked out.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining the neutral grip and stable shoulder position.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells under control back to the starting position at chest level, keeping elbows from flaring excessively.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, focusing on steady, controlled motion and exhaling during the press and inhaling during the lowering phase.
Maintain scapular stability throughout; avoid overarching the lower back or letting the shoulders roll forward.
exercises.description: Supine dumbbell press using a neutral (hammer) grip to press weights from chest to full arm extension; emphasizes the chest while reducing shoulder rotation. Performed on a flat bench with feet planted and core braced.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Front to Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Stand tall (neutral spine) with feet hip-width apart, knees soft and core braced; hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with palms facing your body (standing position). Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Stand tall (neutral spine) with feet hip-width apart, knees soft and core braced; hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with palms facing your body (standing position).
Keep a slight bend in the elbows and maintain the same elbow angle throughout the movement; do not lock the arms.
Exhale and raise the dumbbells straight forward to shoulder height (front raise) with controlled motion — lead with the elbows rather than the hands.
Without pausing, continue the motion in an arc outward so the arms move from front-to-side until the dumbbells reach shoulder height to form a T (lateral raise position).
Pause briefly at the top with shoulders down (do not shrug) and shoulder blades stable.
Inhale and lower the weights back along the same path, first moving from lateral back to front and then down to the starting position at your sides, maintaining control.
Repeat for desired reps, avoiding torso lean, momentum, or swinging; keep the core engaged and neck relaxed throughout.
exercises.description: A standing combination raise that begins as a front dumbbell raise and transitions into a lateral raise, stressing the shoulder delts through the anterior-to-lateral plane; performed slowly to improve shoulder strength and stability.

tips: Every working set should reach true muscular failure.
Use slow, controlled tempo throughout the movement.
Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Fly
exercises.notes: Select an appropriate weight and grasp the handles with a neutral or palms-facing grip, arms out to the sides with a slight bend in the elbows (no locked joints). Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit on the machine with your back firmly against the pad and feet flat on the floor; adjust the seat so the handles are roughly at mid-chest level.
Select an appropriate weight and grasp the handles with a neutral or palms-facing grip, arms out to the sides with a slight bend in the elbows (no locked joints).
Brace your core and keep your chest up and shoulders down and back throughout the movement (seated, back supported).
Exhale and pull the handles together in a smooth arc in front of your chest, leading with the elbows and focusing on squeezing the pectorals at the end range.
Pause briefly at full contraction while maintaining the slight elbow bend and scapula position (do not let shoulders shrug).
Inhale and slowly return the handles to the start position under control, allowing a stretch in the chest but avoiding excessive shoulder extension.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining consistent tempo and full contact with the back pad.
Common form cues: keep elbows slightly bent, avoid using momentum or leaning forward, and do not let wrists or forearms drive the movement—lead with the chest.
exercises.description: Seated lever fly performed on a chest fly machine to isolate and contract the pectorals by bringing the arms together in front of the torso. The movement uses a slight elbow bend and a controlled arc to emphasize chest contraction.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Single Arm Shoulder Press
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height so the handle starts at about shoulder level when seated and select an appropriate weight on the lever stack. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Adjust the seat height so the handle starts at about shoulder level when seated and select an appropriate weight on the lever stack.
Sit upright with your back firmly against the pad, feet flat on the floor, and brace your core to prevent excessive spinal extension.
Grip the handle with one hand (neutral or overhand grip) so your elbow is roughly under or slightly in front of the shoulder; rest your free hand on your abdomen or thigh for balance.
Exhale and press the handle upward and slightly forward in a controlled arc until your arm is nearly straight (avoid locking the elbow or shrugging the shoulder).
Pause briefly at the top with shoulder blades stable, then inhale and slowly lower the handle back down to the starting position under control.
Perform the desired reps with one arm, then switch sides; maintain a steady tempo, keep the wrist neutral, and avoid leaning or twisting the torso.
exercises.description: Seated single-arm shoulder press performed on a lever machine: press the machine handle upward from shoulder height using one arm to work the delts while the opposite arm can rest for stability. Controlled concentric press and eccentric descent emphasize shoulder strength and stability.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

exercises.name: Cable Close Grip Front Lat Pulldown
exercises.notes: Attach a close-grip/V-bar or place hands close together on a narrow bar; grasp the handle with a firm, neutral/pronated grip (hands close together). Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit on the lat pulldown bench and secure your thighs under the pads so your torso cannot rise during the movement (seated position).
Attach a close-grip/V-bar or place hands close together on a narrow bar; grasp the handle with a firm, neutral/pronated grip (hands close together).
Start with arms extended overhead, chest up and a slight lean backward from the hips (about 10–20°) to put tension on the lats (slight recline).
Initiate the pull by driving your elbows down and back, leading with the elbows rather than the hands, and pull the bar to the upper chest/upper sternum.
At the bottom of the movement, squeeze your lats and upper back for a brief 1–2 second pause while keeping the torso stable and not using momentum.
Slowly extend your arms back to the start in a controlled manner until your lats are under tension again (full but not locked extension).
Repeat for the desired number of reps; keep core engaged and avoid excessive swinging or pulling with the lower back.
exercises.description: Seated cable pulldown using a close grip to emphasize the latissimus dorsi while minimizing shoulder abduction; a narrow grip increases biceps and upper-back involvement. Performed seated with thigh pads to stabilize the body while pulling the handle to the upper chest.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Bent Arm Lateral raise
exercises.notes: Hinge your torso forward about 30–45 degrees while keeping your back straight, chest up and neck neutral. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit on the front edge of a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and knees close together; hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms hanging down between your legs.
Hinge your torso forward about 30–45 degrees while keeping your back straight, chest up and neck neutral.
Bend your elbows to about 90 degrees and keep your forearms nearly vertical with palms facing each other (neutral grip).
Exhale and lift the dumbbells out to your sides by raising your elbows, leading with the elbows until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor (shoulder height).
Pause briefly at the top, maintaining the bent-elbow position and avoiding shrugging the shoulders.
Inhale and slowly lower the weights back to the starting position under control, keeping the torso still and avoiding swinging.
Repeat for the desired number of reps; choose a weight that allows controlled motion and focus on feeling the delts work rather than using momentum.
exercises.description: Seated bent-arm lateral raise is a shoulder isolation exercise performed seated with the torso tipped forward and elbows bent, lifting the dumbbells out to the sides to emphasize the lateral deltoids. It minimizes momentum and places more emphasis on the delts and upper scapular muscles.

exercises.name: Cable Pulldown (pro lat bar)
exercises.notes: Select an appropriate weight and grasp the pro-lat bar with a wide, pronated (palms facing away) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit on the pulldown bench with knees secured under the thigh pads and feet flat on the floor (seated, knees braced).
Select an appropriate weight and grasp the pro-lat bar with a wide, pronated (palms facing away) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width.
Begin tall with a neutral spine and a slight lean back from the hips (not from the lower back), chest lifted and eyes forward.
Inhale, then exhale and initiate the movement by pulling the shoulder blades down and together (scapular retraction) to engage the lats.
Pull the bar down in a controlled arc toward the upper chest (sternal area), leading with the elbows and keeping the wrists neutral.
Pause briefly at the bottom and squeeze the lats and upper back without shrugging the shoulders.
Inhale and slowly allow the bar to return to the start position with straight arms, fully extending the shoulders while maintaining a stable torso.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, avoiding excessive torso lean or momentum; keep movement controlled and driven by the lats.
exercises.description: Seated cable pulldown using a pro-lat bar performed from a tall seated position, pulling the bar down to the upper chest to target the latissimus dorsi and upper back. Focus is on scapular retraction and controlled eccentric return.

tips: Every working set should reach true muscular failure.
Use slow, controlled tempo throughout the movement.
Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.

exercises.name: Close Grip Chin-Up
exercises.notes: Stand under a pull-up bar and grasp it with a close, underhand (palms facing you / supinated) grip roughly shoulder-width or slightly narrower; thumbs can wrap around the bar.
instructions: Stand under a pull-up bar and grasp it with a close, underhand (palms facing you / supinated) grip roughly shoulder-width or slightly narrower; thumbs can wrap around the bar.
Step or jump up so you are hanging with arms fully extended, legs either bent at the knees or crossed behind you to avoid touching the floor; maintain a slight hollow-body position with core engaged.
Begin the rep by depressing and slightly retracting the shoulder blades (set the scapula) to engage the lats before pulling with the arms.
Pull your body upward by driving the elbows down and back, leading with the chest and squeezing the lats and upper back until your chin clears the bar.
Pause briefly at the top while keeping the torso stable and the biceps engaged.
Lower yourself slowly and under control back to a full dead hang with arms extended, maintaining tension in the lats and core throughout the descent.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, avoiding kipping or excessive swinging; breathe out while pulling up and inhale while lowering.
exercises.description: A close-grip chin-up is an underhand (supinated) pull-up variation performed with a narrow grip that emphasizes the lats while placing significant demand on the biceps and forearms. The movement involves pulling the body up until the chin clears the bar, then lowering under control to a full hang.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie back so you are supine on the bench, feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and maintain a neutral spine with a slight natural arch; retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together.
instructions: Sit on the edge of a flat bench with a dumbbell resting on each thigh.
Lie back so you are supine on the bench, feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and maintain a neutral spine with a slight natural arch; retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together.
Use your legs and core to help bring the dumbbells up to chest height so your upper arms are about 45 degrees from your torso and palms are facing forward (or slightly inward if preferred).

Inhale, then slowly lower the dumbbells in a controlled manner until your elbows are roughly level with the bench or just below chest level, keeping wrists neutral and elbows tracking slightly tucked.

Pause briefly at the bottom without relaxing the shoulder blade position, then exhale and press the dumbbells upward and slightly together until your arms are almost fully extended — avoid locking the elbows.
Control the descent back to the starting position and repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
To finish the set, bring the dumbbells safely to your knees, sit up, and set them down or have a spotter assist if using heavy weight.
exercises.description: The dumbbell bench press is a supine pressing movement performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and delts for elbow and shoulder extension. Dumbbells allow a greater range of motion and require more stabilization from the forearms and serratus anterior.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

exercises.name: Barbell Pendlay Row
exercises.notes: Grip the bar with a double overhand or mixed grip about shoulder-width apart, keep a neutral spine and eyes looking a few feet ahead on the floor.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and the barbell over mid-foot; bend at the hips and knees to reach the bar so your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (hip hinge), knees slightly bent.
Grip the bar with a double overhand or mixed grip about shoulder-width apart, keep a neutral spine and eyes looking a few feet ahead on the floor.
Brace your core, pull your shoulder blades down and back, then explosively row the bar in a straight line to your lower chest/upper abdomen, driving your elbows back.
Squeeze your upper back at the top for 1 second while keeping the torso stable (no rowing with the hips or excessive torso rotation).
Lower the bar under control all the way back to the floor so it comes to a complete stop, reset any slack, then repeat for the desired reps.
Maintain a flat back and braced core throughout; if your lower back rounds or you must use momentum, reduce weight or raise the bar height.
exercises.description: The Barbell Pendlay Row is a strict horizontal barbell row performed from a dead stop on the floor each rep, emphasizing the upper back and lats while minimizing torso momentum. It uses a hip hinge with a flat back and pulls the bar to the lower chest/upper abdomen before returning it to the floor.

exercises.name: Landmine Shoulders Press with Viking Press Attachment
exercises.notes: Stand facing the landmine with feet shoulder-width apart and a slight bend in the knees; grip the Viking press handles so the attachment sits at shoulder height with elbows roughly tucked under the wrists.
instructions: Stand facing the landmine with feet shoulder-width apart and a slight bend in the knees; grip the Viking press handles so the attachment sits at shoulder height with elbows roughly tucked under the wrists.
Brace your core, keep your chest up and neutral spine, and position the bar angled away from you so it can travel upward without hitting your head.
Inhale, then press the handles upward and slightly forward in a controlled arc until your arms are fully extended overhead or slightly behind the head, avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Pause briefly at the top with shoulders engaged and elbows unlocked but not hyperextended.
Exhale as you slowly lower the handles back down to the starting shoulder-height position under control, maintaining tension in the delts and core.
Repeat for the desired reps; keep movement smooth and controlled, avoid leaning the torso excessively back or using lower-body drive to move the load.
Adjust stance or hand position to shift emphasis (wider grip = more lateral delt emphasis; closer grip = more anterior delt/pectorals) and lower the weight if you cannot maintain strict form.
exercises.description: A standing landmine press using a Viking press attachment that emphasizes overhead pressing from the shoulders; the movement drives the handles upward from shoulder height to full arm extension, targeting the delts while the triceps and upper chest assist.

exercises.name: Barbell Decline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set a decline bench into a rack and place a barbell at a height you can safely unrack while lying down; secure your feet under the decline foot pads.
instructions: Set a decline bench into a rack and place a barbell at a height you can safely unrack while lying down; secure your feet under the decline foot pads.
Lie supine on the decline bench so your head is lower than your hips and your shoulders and back are flat against the bench; keep feet locked under the pads for stability.
Grasp the bar with a double overhand grip slightly wider than shoulder width (pronated grip); wrists stacked over elbows and shoulders pulled down and back.
Unrack the bar by straightening your arms and moving the bar out over your lower chest; maintain a controlled, tight torso and scapular retraction.
Inhale and slowly lower the bar in a controlled path to the lower chest/upper abdomen (sternal/xyphoid area) with elbows at roughly a 30–45° angle from the torso.
Pause briefly with the bar touching or just above the chest, then exhale and press the bar upward along the same path until arms are nearly extended.
Avoid flaring the elbows excessively and keep the feet anchored under the pads; use a spotter for heavy sets and re-rack the bar carefully after the set.
exercises.description: A decline barbell bench press performed lying supine on a decline bench with the head lower than the hips to emphasize the lower portion of the pectorals. The lifter lowers the bar to the lower chest/upper abdomen and presses it back up to full extension.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

exercises.name: Barbell Incline Row
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and place a loaded barbell within reach on the floor or on low supports.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and place a loaded barbell within reach on the floor or on low supports.
Lie face down (prone) on the bench so your chest is supported, feet braced on the floor or bench for stability, and your torso is fixed against the pad.
Reach down and grasp the barbell with an overhand (pronated) grip about shoulder-width apart. Allow your arms to hang fully extended.
Before pulling, draw your shoulder blades down and together (scapular retraction) to start the movement from the back rather than the arms.
Pull the barbell upward in a controlled path toward your lower chest/upper abdomen by driving your elbows back and slightly out.
At the top, squeeze your shoulder blades together and hold briefly (about 1 second) while keeping the torso pressed to the bench and neck neutral.
Lower the barbell slowly back to full arm extension under control, maintaining scapular tension and avoiding any bouncing or trunk rotation.
Breathe out during the pull and inhale while lowering. Keep movement strict—do not jerk the weight or lift your chest off the bench.
exercises.description: Prone row performed on an incline bench where the torso is supported and a barbell is pulled toward the lower chest/upper abdomen. It emphasizes the latissimus dorsi while also engaging the upper back, rear delts and arm muscles.

exercises.name: Reverse grip Pull-up
exercises.notes: Set up: grasp a pull-up bar with a supinated (underhand) grip, hands about shoulder-width apart or slightly narrower.
instructions: Set up: grasp a pull-up bar with a supinated (underhand) grip, hands about shoulder-width apart or slightly narrower.
Start position: hang fully extended with feet off the ground (legs can be crossed at the ankles or slightly bent) and scapula slightly depressed and retracted to remove slack.
Initiate the pull by driving the elbows down and back, engaging the lats and upper back rather than just curling with the arms.
Continue pulling until your chin passes the bar or your upper chest reaches the bar, keeping the torso as upright and controlled as possible.
Pause briefly at the top while squeezing the lats and upper back, avoiding excessive neck or shoulder shrugging.
Lower slowly and under control back to a full dead hang (arms fully extended) while maintaining scapular control.
Repeat for the desired number of reps. Breathe out on the pull and inhale as you lower. Avoid kipping or excessive swinging to keep tension on the target muscles.
exercises.description: A supinated (underhand) grip pull-up that emphasizes the latissimus and recruits the biceps more than a pronated pull-up. From a dead hang you pull your torso up until your chin or chest reaches the bar, then lower with control.

exercises.name: Barbell Close Grip Larsen Press
exercises.notes: Set a barbell in a rack at a height you can safely unrack while lying on a flat bench; load appropriate weight and use a spotter or safety pins.
instructions: Set a barbell in a rack at a height you can safely unrack while lying on a flat bench; load appropriate weight and use a spotter or safety pins.
Lie supine on the bench with your eyes roughly under the bar, feet planted flat on the floor for stability, and shoulder blades squeezed together.
Grip the bar with a close/narrow grip (hands about shoulder-width or slightly narrower) with thumbs wrapped around the bar.
Unrack the bar and hold it with arms extended over the sternum, wrists stacked over elbows and bar positioned vertically above your chest.
Lower the bar under control to the upper-to-mid chest (sternal/clavicular area) while keeping elbows tucked closer to your body to protect the shoulders.
Allow the bar to lightly rest on the chest (brief pause) — this is the Larsen touch-and-rest — then drive the bar upward by pressing through the chest and triceps until arms are nearly extended.
Keep a tight torso and steady breathing: inhale on the descent, exhale as you press up. Repeat for desired reps.
Re‑rack the bar under control at the end of the set, making sure the bar is fully seated on the rack supports.
exercises.description: A close‑grip variation of the bench press where the bar is briefly rested on the chest (Larsen style) between reps; emphasizes the inner chest while also heavily recruiting the triceps and front delts. Performed lying supine on a flat bench using a narrow hand position.

exercises.name: Hammer Grip Pull-up on Dip Cage
exercises.notes: Stand beneath the dip cage and grasp the parallel/neutral handles with a hammer grip (palms facing each other), hands about shoulder-width apart; step or jump up until arms are fully extended and you are in a full hang.
instructions: Stand beneath the dip cage and grasp the parallel/neutral handles with a hammer grip (palms facing each other), hands about shoulder-width apart; step or jump up until arms are fully extended and you are in a full hang. Cross or bend your legs behind you if needed to avoid touching the floor.
Brace your core and begin by depressing and retracting the shoulder blades (scapular pull) to engage the lats before bending the elbows.

Pull your body upward by driving the elbows down and back toward your ribs, leading with the chest until your chin clears the bar or your chest reaches the handles; keep the torso relatively upright and avoid excessive swinging or kipping.
Hold the top position briefly with shoulders packed (down and back), then slowly lower yourself under control back to a full dead hang with arms fully extended.
Breathe out as you pull up and inhale as you lower; maintain a steady tempo and repeat for the desired number of reps, focusing on lat contraction rather than momentum.
exercises.description: A neutral (hammer) grip pull-up performed on a dip/pull-up station that emphasizes the lats while reducing strain on the shoulders by using palms-facing-each-other handles. Performed from a dead hang and pulling the chest toward the bar with controlled descent.

exercises.name: Shoulder Grip Pull-up
exercises.notes: Stand under a pull-up bar and reach up to grip it with an overhand (palms facing away) shoulder-width grip.
instructions: Stand under a pull-up bar and reach up to grip it with an overhand (palms facing away) shoulder-width grip.
Begin in a dead hang with arms fully extended, legs either straight or lightly crossed at the ankles, core braced and shoulders slightly depressed (scapulae down and back).
Initiate the movement by pulling your shoulder blades down and together, then drive your elbows down and back while squeezing the lats to lift your chest toward the bar.
Continue pulling until your chin clears the bar (or chest approaches the bar), keeping the torso relatively upright and avoiding excessive swinging or kipping.
Lower yourself back to a full dead hang in a slow, controlled manner until arms are fully extended and scapulae are back to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of reps — exhale during the upward (concentric) phase and inhale as you lower (eccentric) — maintain tension in the core and avoid shrugging the shoulders at the top.
exercises.description: A shoulder-width overhand pull-up where you hang from the bar and pull your body up using your back and arms. Focuses on vertical pulling to develop the lats while the biceps and upper back assist.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.
