exercises.name: Cable Standing Chest Press
exercises.notes: Set the pulleys to chest height and attach single-handle grips to each side.
instructions: Set the pulleys to chest height and attach single-handle grips to each side.
Stand facing away from the machine and grab a handle in each hand with a neutral or palms-down grip; bring the handles to chest level with elbows bent and hands near the sternum.
Step forward into a staggered or shoulder-width stance so one foot is slightly in front of the other; brace your core and keep a slight bend in the knees.
Keep shoulders down and back, chest up, and press both handles forward and inward until your arms are almost fully extended (do not lock the elbows).
Exhale as you press; hold the end position briefly while keeping tension on the cables.
Inhale and slowly control the return of the handles back to the starting position at chest level, allowing the shoulder blades to set but not excessively retract.
Repeat for the desired reps, maintaining a stable torso and avoiding leaning forward or using excessive lower-back arching.
exercises.description: A standing cable press that targets the chest by pressing handles forward from chest level against cable resistance; the movement also engages the anterior delts and triceps while the core stabilizes the torso. Performed facing away from the cable column with a staggered stance for balance.

exercises.name: Dumbbell 2 Point Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with your upper back and shoulders supported and one foot planted firmly on the floor near the bench.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with your upper back and shoulders supported and one foot planted firmly on the floor near the bench.

Bend the opposite knee and lift that foot off the floor so only one foot is providing support (single‑leg position); press your hips up into a bridge so your torso forms a straight line from shoulder to knee and brace your abs.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand (or a single dumbbell if using one) at chest level with a neutral grip or palms facing forward, elbows bent and tucked at about a 45° angle to your torso.
Inhale, then press the weight straight up by extending your arms until elbows are nearly locked and the dumbbells are over your chest while keeping hips elevated and core tight.
Pause briefly at the top, keeping shoulders stable on the bench and hips high, then slowly lower the dumbbells back to your chest under control while maintaining the bridge position.
Complete the prescribed reps on one leg, then carefully lower your hips, switch legs, and repeat on the other side. Keep movement controlled and avoid hyperextending the lower back.
exercises.description: A bench press performed while holding a hip bridge (single-leg position) to add core and glute stability demand; you press the dumbbells from chest to full arm extension while maintaining an elevated hip. This combines chest pressing with unilateral lower‑body stabilization.

exercises.name: Cable Palm Rotational Row
exercises.notes: Set a single handle on a low or mid cable pulley.
instructions: Set a single handle on a low or mid cable pulley. Select a weight you can control for the full range of motion.
Stand facing the machine with feet about hip‑width apart, knees slightly bent. Hinge forward at the hips so your torso is angled forward (approximately 30–45°), maintaining a neutral spine.
Reach forward and grasp the handle with the working hand; start with the arm extended and the palm in the starting orientation (e.g., palm down or neutral). Brace your core to prevent torso rotation.
Initiate the row by pulling the handle toward your torso while simultaneously rotating your palm (rotate the wrist/forearm) so the hand ends in the inward/neutral position as you finish the pull.
Keep the elbow close to the body and lead the motion with the elbow and shoulder blade—squeeze the shoulder blades together at the peak contraction to target the upper back.
Hold the contracted position briefly (1–2 seconds), then slowly extend the arm forward while reversing the palm rotation back to the start, controlling the cable on the return.
Repeat for the desired reps, then switch sides. Maintain a stable, hip‑hinged standing position throughout and avoid using torso momentum or excessive lower back arch.
exercises.description: A single‑arm cable row performed from a standing, hip‑hinged position where the hand rotates (palm rotation) during the pull to emphasize the upper back and rear shoulder musculature. The movement combines a horizontal rowing action with forearm/palm rotation to increase upper‑back and rear‑delt engagement.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.
instructions: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.

Lie supine on the bench with your head, upper back and glutes in contact with the bench, feet flat on the floor and a small natural arch in the lower back; retract and depress your shoulder blades to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists should be stacked over the elbows and forearms vertical when the bar is over the chest.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar out over the chest while keeping the scapulae stable; lock the elbows briefly before starting the set.
Inhale and lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-chest/sternum (around nipple level), keeping the elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso to protect the shoulders.
Allow the bar to touch or come very close to the chest without bouncing; maintain tension in the pectorals and keep the feet and glutes braced.
Exhale and press the bar upward explosively while keeping the bar path slightly arced toward the hips until the arms are fully extended but not hyperextended.
Pause briefly at the top with the shoulders stable, then begin the next rep. Maintain consistent form and breathing throughout the set.
Use a spotter or safety pins in the rack for heavy sets, and avoid flaring the elbows excessively or letting the lower back lift off the bench.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a compound pressing movement performed lying supine on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and anterior delts. It involves lowering a loaded barbell to the chest and pressing it back up to full arm extension with controlled technique.

exercises.name: Barbell Pullover To Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and hips down (bench‑supported supine position); hold a barbell with a double overhand grip roughly shoulder‑width apart.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and hips down (bench‑supported supine position); hold a barbell with a double overhand grip roughly shoulder‑width apart.
Start with the barbell held vertically over your chest with elbows slightly bent and core braced to maintain a neutral spine.

Inhale and slowly lower the barbell in an arc behind your head by flexing at the shoulders, keeping the upper arms angled and elbows soft until you feel a stretch across the lats/pectorals (do not let the lower back over-arch).
Pause briefly at the bottom of the pullover stretch while maintaining core tension and controlled breathing.
Drive the barbell back in a controlled arc over the chest by contracting the lats and pectorals; as the bar reaches chest level, transition into the press.
Exhale and press the barbell straight up until arms are fully extended overhead, using shoulders and triceps to finish the movement while keeping the ribs down and abs engaged.
Slowly lower the barbell back down to the chest under control to reset for the next rep, maintaining a steady rhythm and avoiding excessive momentum.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions. Use a manageable weight to protect the shoulders and maintain proper form throughout.
exercises.description: A supine combination movement that begins as a barbell pullover (bringing the weight in an arc behind the head to stretch and work the lats) and finishes as an overhead press, adding shoulder and triceps work. It trains lat length and upper‑body pressing strength while requiring core stabilization.

exercises.name: Lever One Arm Lateral High Row
exercises.notes: Adjust the machine seat and chest/back pad so the handle is reachable with your arm extended overhead while seated; select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Adjust the machine seat and chest/back pad so the handle is reachable with your arm extended overhead while seated; select an appropriate weight.
Sit upright with your chest braced against the pad, feet flat on the floor, core engaged and torso stable to prevent twisting.
Reach up and grasp the single high handle with one hand (neutral or pronated grip) while keeping the shoulder down and away from the ear.
Initiate the movement by driving the elbow down and back in a lateral arc, leading with the elbow so the hand moves toward the side of your ribs and the shoulder blade retracts and rotates downward.
Squeeze the upper back (scapulae) at the end range for 1–2 seconds while keeping the torso still and avoiding excessive shoulder elevation or rotation.
Slowly return the handle to the start position with control, fully extending the arm while maintaining tension in the working muscles.
Perform the desired reps on one side, then switch arms and repeat. Maintain steady breathing (exhale while pulling, inhale while returning).
exercises.description: A single-arm high pulley/lever row performed from a seated position that pulls the handle laterally and upward to emphasize the upper back and rear shoulder musculature. It focuses on scapular retraction and elbow-led pulling to target the upper back and lats while the arm and grip work the biceps and forearms.

exercises.name: Assisted Pull-up
exercises.notes: Set the assistance weight on the machine so you can complete the desired reps with good form.
instructions: Set the assistance weight on the machine so you can complete the desired reps with good form.
Kneel on the machine's knee pad (or place knees on the assistance platform) and reach up to grasp the pull-up bar with a shoulder-width, pronated (overhand) grip; keep head neutral.
Before pulling, pull your shoulder blades down and back (scapular retraction) to engage the lats and upper back.
Pull your chest upward toward the bar by driving the elbows down and back, keeping the torso relatively upright and avoiding excessive swinging.
Pause briefly at the top with your chin over the bar, maintaining scapular retraction.
Slowly lower yourself back to the starting position until your arms are fully extended, maintaining control throughout the eccentric phase.
Repeat for the prescribed number of repetitions, and adjust assistance if reps become too easy or too difficult.
exercises.description: Kneeling on an assisted pull-up machine, the exerciser pulls their torso up toward an overhead bar using a counterweight to reduce load; this movement primarily trains the latissimus dorsi and upper back pulling muscles. Control the descent to emphasize the eccentric phase.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Lying One Arm Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and knees bent to stabilize the lower body.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and knees bent to stabilize the lower body.
Hold a dumbbell in one hand at chest level with a firm grip (palm facing forward or neutral); place your free hand on your chest or the bench for balance.
Retract your shoulder blade slightly on the working side and brace your core to prevent your torso from rotating.
Exhale and press the dumbbell straight up until the arm is nearly extended, keeping a soft bend in the elbow (do not fully lock out).
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbell under control back to the chest level, keeping the elbow at about a 45-degree angle relative to the torso.
Maintain steady posture and avoid twisting; reset your scapula and core between reps if needed.
Complete the desired reps on one side, then switch arms and repeat with equal control and setup.
exercises.description: A single-arm dumbbell bench press performed lying supine on a flat bench to train the pectorals unilaterally while the triceps and delts assist; the opposite hand rests for stability. Emphasizes controlled pressing and core stabilization to prevent torso rotation.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Straight Arm Pullover
exercises.notes: Brace your core and keep your hips stable (avoid excessive lower-back arch); inhale and slowly lower the dumbbell in a controlled arc behind your head until you feel a deep stretch in the lats/upper chest or your range limit.
instructions: Position yourself supine on a flat bench with your upper back and shoulders supported, feet flat on the floor and knees bent; hold one dumbbell with both hands (palms under one end) above your chest with arms nearly straight and a slight bend in the elbows.

Brace your core and keep your hips stable (avoid excessive lower-back arch); inhale and slowly lower the dumbbell in a controlled arc behind your head until you feel a deep stretch in the lats/upper chest or your range limit.
Pause briefly at the bottom while maintaining the slight elbow bend and scapular stability (do not shrug or let the shoulders round forward).
Exhale and pull the dumbbell back in the same arc to the start position above your chest by driving the motion with the lats and ribcage expansion, keeping the arms mostly straight.
Repeat for the desired reps with slow, controlled tempo; avoid using momentum, excessive elbow bend, or letting the lower back hyperextend.
exercises.description: A supine dumbbell pullover performed with mostly straight arms that moves the weight in an arc from over the chest to behind the head, emphasizing a long-lever stretch and concentric pull to work the lats and upper chest.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

exercises.name: Cable Rear Pulldown
exercises.notes: Adjust the thigh pads so your legs are secured and select an appropriate weight on the stack.
instructions: Adjust the thigh pads so your legs are secured and select an appropriate weight on the stack.
Sit upright on the bench with feet flat, chest up and head in a neutral position; reach overhead and grasp a wide curved lat bar with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder width.
Lean back very slightly at the hips (small forward lean is acceptable for comfort), keep the chest lifted and scapulae retracted before initiating the pull.
Exhale and pull the bar down behind your head toward the upper trapezius/occipital area by driving the elbows down and back, focusing on squeezing the lats and shoulder blades together.
Pause briefly at the bottom with control, avoiding excessive neck extension or forward head movement.
Inhale and slowly allow the bar to return to the fully extended start position with controlled tension in the lats; do not let the weight stack slam.
Repeat for the desired reps. Use a front pulldown variation instead if you have shoulder pain or limited mobility to reduce risk.
exercises.description: Seated cable pulldown where the bar is pulled down behind the head to target the large back muscles. Emphasizes the lats and upper posterior shoulder/upper back while the arms act as secondary drivers.

exercises.name: Lever Outdoor Seated Chest Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on the machine seat with your back firmly against the back pad and feet flat on the ground.
instructions: Sit upright on the machine seat with your back firmly against the back pad and feet flat on the ground.
Adjust the seat height so the handles are aligned with the mid-chest (nipple line) when you grip them.
Grasp the handles with a neutral or pronated grip, elbows bent at about 80–90° and held slightly below shoulder height.
Brace your core, keep your chest up and shoulders down/back, and exhale as you press the handles forward and slightly together until your arms are nearly extended (do not lock the elbows).
Pause briefly at full extension, maintaining control and a stable torso.
Inhale and slowly return the handles to the starting position, letting the chest stretch but keeping tension on the muscles; avoid letting the weight slam or your shoulders roll forward.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining a steady tempo and full shoulder-back contact with the pad throughout the set.
exercises.description: Seated lever chest press performed on an outdoor machine where you push the handles away from the chest to work the pectoral muscles. The movement uses the delts and triceps as secondary movers while you maintain a stable, seated position against the pad.

exercises.name: Lever Bent over Row (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Set the lever machine or plate-loaded bar to the desired weight and stand with feet hip-width under the bar/platform.
instructions: Set the lever machine or plate-loaded bar to the desired weight and stand with feet hip-width under the bar/platform.
Hinge at the hips with a soft bend in the knees until your torso is approximately 30–45 degrees from vertical (bent-over position); keep the spine neutral and neck in line with the torso.
Grasp the bar with an overhand (or chosen) grip about shoulder-width apart and let your arms hang straight down; engage your core to stabilize the trunk.
Initiate the rep by pulling your elbows back and driving the bar toward your lower chest/upper abdomen, squeezing the shoulder blades together at the top of the movement.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining a braced torso, then slowly lower the bar back to the start under control until the arms are extended.
Repeat for the desired reps, avoiding torso rotation, excessive momentum, or rounding of the lower back throughout the set.
exercises.description: A bent-over lever row performed from a hip-hinged position to drive the loaded bar toward the torso, emphasizing the mid/upper back. It develops pulling strength and upper-back thickness while also engaging the arms and rear shoulders.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Reverse Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and a slight natural arch in the lower back.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and a slight natural arch in the lower back.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with a reverse (supinated) grip so your palms face toward your head; retract your shoulder blades and keep your chest lifted.
Start with the dumbbells at chest level, elbows tucked roughly 30–45 degrees from your torso and forearms vertical.
Inhale, then press the dumbbells upward and slightly back in a controlled path until your arms are extended but not aggressively hyperextended at the elbows.
Exhale as you reach the top, maintain wrist alignment so the weight sits over the forearms, and keep the shoulders down and back.
Slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting chest position under control, feeling the stretch in the pectorals while keeping the reverse grip and elbow position.
Repeat for the desired reps. Use a spotter for heavier loads and avoid excessive wrist extension or flaring of the elbows.
exercises.description: A supine dumbbell press performed with a reverse (supinated) grip that emphasizes the upper/inner pectorals and increases biceps and forearm involvement compared with a standard press.

exercises.name: Cable Straight Back Seated Row
exercises.notes: Select an appropriate weight, reach forward and grasp the handle with a neutral or double-handed grip, arms extended and spine straight (no rounded back).
instructions: Sit on the bench or seat facing the cable machine with feet braced on the footplate and knees slightly bent.
Select an appropriate weight, reach forward and grasp the handle with a neutral or double-handed grip, arms extended and spine straight (no rounded back).
Engage your core, maintain an upright torso (straight back), and initiate the movement by pulling your shoulder blades together (scapular retraction).
Drive your elbows straight back toward your torso while keeping them close to your sides until the handle touches your abdomen or lower chest.
Pause and squeeze your lats/upper back at the end range for 1–2 seconds while avoiding excessive torso lean or trunk rotation.
Slowly extend your arms and allow the cable to return to the start position under control, keeping a straight back throughout the eccentric phase.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, breathing out on the pull and breathing in on the return.
exercises.description: A seated cable row performed with an upright, straight back to pull a handle toward the torso, emphasizing scapular retraction and elbow drive to work the mid- and lower-back muscles. Focus is on controlled pulling and resisting the cable on the return.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bent over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips until your torso is leaning forward (torso roughly parallel to the floor or about 30–45° above parallel), knees slightly bent; this is a bent-over position — brace your core and keep a neutral spine.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral (palms facing) grip.

Hinge at the hips until your torso is leaning forward (torso roughly parallel to the floor or about 30–45° above parallel), knees slightly bent; this is a bent-over position — brace your core and keep a neutral spine.
Let the dumbbells hang straight down from your shoulders with your arms fully extended; keep your head aligned with your spine.
Initiate the pull by driving your elbows back and up, pulling the dumbbells toward your lower ribs or hip area while squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Pause briefly at the top of the movement, maintaining tightness through the lats and upper back without shrugging the shoulders excessively.
Slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position with controlled motion until your arms are extended.
Repeat for the prescribed reps, keeping the torso fixed (avoid using momentum or excessive torso rotation) and continue to breathe: exhale while pulling, inhale while lowering.
exercises.description: A bent-over row performed with dumbbells where you hinge at the hips and pull the weights toward your torso, emphasizing the lats and upper back. The movement also engages the arms and posterior chain to stabilize the hinged position.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Lying One Arm Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and knees bent to stabilize the lower body.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and knees bent to stabilize the lower body.
Hold a dumbbell in one hand at chest level with a firm grip (palm facing forward or neutral); place your free hand on your chest or the bench for balance.
Retract your shoulder blade slightly on the working side and brace your core to prevent your torso from rotating.
Exhale and press the dumbbell straight up until the arm is nearly extended, keeping a soft bend in the elbow (do not fully lock out).
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbell under control back to the chest level, keeping the elbow at about a 45-degree angle relative to the torso.
Maintain steady posture and avoid twisting; reset your scapula and core between reps if needed.
Complete the desired reps on one side, then switch arms and repeat with equal control and setup.
exercises.description: A single-arm dumbbell bench press performed lying supine on a flat bench to train the pectorals unilaterally while the triceps and delts assist; the opposite hand rests for stability. Emphasizes controlled pressing and core stabilization to prevent torso rotation.

exercises.name: Cable Pulldown (pro lat bar)
exercises.notes: Select an appropriate weight and grasp the pro-lat bar with a wide, pronated (palms facing away) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width.
instructions: Sit on the pulldown bench with knees secured under the thigh pads and feet flat on the floor (seated, knees braced).
Select an appropriate weight and grasp the pro-lat bar with a wide, pronated (palms facing away) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width.
Begin tall with a neutral spine and a slight lean back from the hips (not from the lower back), chest lifted and eyes forward.
Inhale, then exhale and initiate the movement by pulling the shoulder blades down and together (scapular retraction) to engage the lats.
Pull the bar down in a controlled arc toward the upper chest (sternal area), leading with the elbows and keeping the wrists neutral.
Pause briefly at the bottom and squeeze the lats and upper back without shrugging the shoulders.
Inhale and slowly allow the bar to return to the start position with straight arms, fully extending the shoulders while maintaining a stable torso.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, avoiding excessive torso lean or momentum; keep movement controlled and driven by the lats.
exercises.description: Seated cable pulldown using a pro-lat bar performed from a tall seated position, pulling the bar down to the upper chest to target the latissimus dorsi and upper back. Focus is on scapular retraction and controlled eccentric return.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

exercises.name: Cable Underhand Pulldown
exercises.notes: Set the seat height and thigh pads so your knees are secured under the pad and select an appropriate weight; attach a straight bar to the high pulley.
instructions: Set the seat height and thigh pads so your knees are secured under the pad and select an appropriate weight; attach a straight bar to the high pulley.
Sit upright on the bench with feet flat, torso tall and slightly braced, then reach up and grasp the bar with an underhand (supinated) grip about shoulder-width apart.
Begin with arms fully extended and scapulae slightly protracted (shoulders relaxed up), inhale, then exhale as you pull the bar down by driving your elbows down and back.
Pull the bar to the upper chest/clavicle area while keeping the torso stable and leaning back only slightly; focus on contracting the lats and squeezing the shoulder blades together.
Pause briefly at the bottom, then slowly return the bar under control to the start position with arms nearly straight and a controlled stretch in the lats.
Repeat for the desired reps, avoiding torso momentum or excessive backward lean; keep wrists neutral and core engaged throughout.
exercises.description: Seated cable underhand pulldown (reverse-grip pulldown) is a vertical pulling exercise that emphasizes the latissimus dorsi while using a supinated grip to increase biceps involvement. Performed seated at a high pulley, the bar is pulled down to the upper chest with elbows driven down and back.

exercises.name: Cable Front Seated Row
exercises.notes: Adjust the cable pulley to chest height and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Adjust the cable pulley to chest height and select an appropriate weight.
Sit on the bench facing the machine with your chest pressed against the vertical support pad, feet flat on the floor and knees bent at about 90°.
Reach forward and grasp the handle with a neutral or overhand grip, arms fully extended and shoulders relaxed.
Keep your torso upright and core braced; maintain contact with the chest pad throughout the movement.
Exhale and pull the handle straight back by driving your elbows toward your ribcage, leading with the elbows and squeezing the shoulder blades together.
Pause briefly at the end range when your hands reach your torso and you feel a full contraction across the upper back.
Inhale and slowly extend your arms forward in a controlled manner until they are nearly straight, resisting the weight on the return.
Repeat for the desired reps, avoiding trunk rotation, excessive trunk lean, or shrugging the shoulders; keep the motion controlled and driven by the back, not the arms.
exercises.description: A seated cable row variation performed facing the machine with chest supported by a pad, pulling a single handle straight toward the torso to work the mid/upper back. Emphasis is on scapular retraction and elbow drive to target the upper back and lats.

exercises.name: Lever Decline Chest Press
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat/back pad so that when seated your back is fully supported, your head is lower than your hips (declined position), and the handles sit roughly at the lower-to-mid chest level.
instructions: Adjust the seat/back pad so that when seated your back is fully supported, your head is lower than your hips (declined position), and the handles sit roughly at the lower-to-mid chest level.
Load an appropriate amount of weight on the machine, or set the pin to the desired resistance if using a selectorized unit.
Sit down with your back and head pressed firmly against the pad, feet flat on the foot platform, and chest up. Grip the handles with a neutral or slightly pronated grip, wrists in line with forearms.
Start with elbows bent and the handles near your lower chest. Brace your core and keep your shoulders down and back (avoid excessive shrugging).
Exhale and press the handles forward and slightly downward in a controlled arc until your arms are almost fully extended; avoid locking the elbows at the top.

Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the pectorals, then inhale and slowly return the handles to the start position, controlling the descent so the chest stretches but shoulders do not flare forward.
Perform the desired number of repetitions while keeping constant tension on the chest, maintaining back contact with the pad, and avoiding excessive arching or momentum.
When finished, return the handles to the safety/starting position, release the grips, and step away from the machine before unloading weight if necessary.
exercises.description: A machine-based decline chest press that emphasizes the lower portion of the pectoral muscles by pressing the handles forward and slightly downward while seated on a declined pad. The fixed lever path provides stability and isolates the chest while also engaging the front delts and triceps.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Every working set should reach true muscular failure.

exercises.name: Barbell Feet Flat Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set the bench inside a rack so the bar sits at arm height when you lie down; load the bar and secure collars or have a spotter.
instructions: Set the bench inside a rack so the bar sits at arm height when you lie down; load the bar and secure collars or have a spotter.
Lie supine on the flat bench with your eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and planted for stability, and a slight natural arch in the lower back.
Retract and depress your shoulder blades (pinch them together and press them into the bench) to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the bar with a full grip slightly wider than shoulder width, wrists stacked over forearms and elbows under the bar.
Unrack the bar by straightening your arms and moving the bar into the start position directly above your shoulders.
Inhale, then lower the bar in a controlled manner to mid-chest / nipple line, keeping elbows at about a 45° angle to the torso and maintaining scapular retraction.
Briefly pause with the bar lightly contacting the chest (do not bounce), then exhale and press the bar upward along a slightly arced path back to full arm extension.
Lock out the elbows at the top without overextending, keep the feet planted and core braced throughout the set.
After the final rep, carefully rack the bar onto the supports or have your spotter assist; use a spotter for heavy loads and maintain strict control on every rep.
exercises.description: Barbell feet-flat flat bench press is a supine pressing exercise that primarily works the pectorals by pressing a loaded barbell from the chest to full arm extension. Keeping the feet flat provides a stable base while the triceps and delts assist in elbow extension and shoulder stabilization.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.
instructions: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.

Lie supine on the bench with your head, upper back and glutes in contact with the bench, feet flat on the floor and a small natural arch in the lower back; retract and depress your shoulder blades to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists should be stacked over the elbows and forearms vertical when the bar is over the chest.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar out over the chest while keeping the scapulae stable; lock the elbows briefly before starting the set.
Inhale and lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-chest/sternum (around nipple level), keeping the elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso to protect the shoulders.
Allow the bar to touch or come very close to the chest without bouncing; maintain tension in the pectorals and keep the feet and glutes braced.
Exhale and press the bar upward explosively while keeping the bar path slightly arced toward the hips until the arms are fully extended but not hyperextended.
Pause briefly at the top with the shoulders stable, then begin the next rep. Maintain consistent form and breathing throughout the set.
Use a spotter or safety pins in the rack for heavy sets, and avoid flaring the elbows excessively or letting the lower back lift off the bench.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a compound pressing movement performed lying supine on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and anterior delts. It involves lowering a loaded barbell to the chest and pressing it back up to full arm extension with controlled technique.

exercises.name: Cable High Row with Chest Support
exercises.notes: Set two cable pulleys to a high position and attach single-hand or D handles; choose a moderate weight.
instructions: Set two cable pulleys to a high position and attach single-hand or D handles; choose a moderate weight.

Position an incline bench or chest pad in front of the machine so you can sit facing the pulleys with your chest pressed firmly against the pad and feet flat on the floor; adjust pad height so torso is supported and arms can reach the handles overhead.
Sit tall with chest against the pad, neutral neck, core braced and shoulders down away from ears; reach up and grasp the handles with a neutral or overhand grip.
Begin with arms nearly extended overhead, scapulae slightly protracted; inhale to prepare.
Exhale and pull the handles down and back toward the upper chest by driving your elbows down and out, leading with the elbows and squeezing the shoulder blades together at the end range.
Hold the contracted position briefly (1–2 seconds) while maintaining a supported chest and no torso rotation.
Slowly release the handles under control, allowing the arms to extend back to the start while keeping tension on the cables and scapulae engaged.
Repeat for desired reps; avoid using momentum, leaning away from the pad, or shrugging the shoulders upward.
exercises.description: Seated chest-supported cable high row performed from dual high pulleys; you pull the handles down and back toward the upper chest while the chest rests against the pad to isolate the upper back. Emphasizes scapular retraction and posterior shoulder activation with reduced lower-back involvement.

exercises.name: Cable Split Stance Single Arm Row
exercises.notes: Set the cable pulley to about chest height and attach a single handle; select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Set the cable pulley to about chest height and attach a single handle; select an appropriate weight.
Stand facing the machine and take a split stance (one foot forward, one foot back) so you are stable; keep knees soft and weight evenly distributed between both legs.
Reach forward and grab the handle with a neutral or overhand grip on the working side; torso should be upright with a slight hip hinge, neutral spine, and eyes forward.
Start with the working arm extended and the scapula slightly protracted; brace your core (engage abs) to prevent torso rotation.
Exhale and pull the handle back by driving the elbow toward your ribcage while actively retracting the shoulder blade—lead with the elbow rather than the hand.
Squeeze the lats and upper-back (scapular) muscles at the end of the concentric phase; keep the shoulder down and avoid shrugging.
Inhale and slowly extend the arm back to the start position under control, allowing the scapula to protract but maintaining a stable torso.
Perform the desired reps, then switch sides and repeat with the opposite arm and reversed split stance to maintain balance.
exercises.description: A single-arm cable rowing variation performed from a split stance that emphasizes unilateral lat activation while challenging core stability and upper-back musculature. The split stance increases balance and prevents torso rotation so the lats and scapular retractors do the work.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bent over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips until your torso is leaning forward (torso roughly parallel to the floor or about 30–45° above parallel), knees slightly bent; this is a bent-over position — brace your core and keep a neutral spine.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral (palms facing) grip.

Hinge at the hips until your torso is leaning forward (torso roughly parallel to the floor or about 30–45° above parallel), knees slightly bent; this is a bent-over position — brace your core and keep a neutral spine.
Let the dumbbells hang straight down from your shoulders with your arms fully extended; keep your head aligned with your spine.
Initiate the pull by driving your elbows back and up, pulling the dumbbells toward your lower ribs or hip area while squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Pause briefly at the top of the movement, maintaining tightness through the lats and upper back without shrugging the shoulders excessively.
Slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position with controlled motion until your arms are extended.
Repeat for the prescribed reps, keeping the torso fixed (avoid using momentum or excessive torso rotation) and continue to breathe: exhale while pulling, inhale while lowering.
exercises.description: A bent-over row performed with dumbbells where you hinge at the hips and pull the weights toward your torso, emphasizing the lats and upper back. The movement also engages the arms and posterior chain to stabilize the hinged position.

exercises.name: Cable Pushdown (straight arm)
exercises.notes: Attach a straight bar, rope, or single handle to a high pulley.
instructions: Attach a straight bar, rope, or single handle to a high pulley. Select a moderate weight that allows controlled movement while keeping arms mostly straight.
Stand facing the machine with feet hip-width apart (or a slight stagger for balance). Grasp the handle with both hands (pronated or neutral grip) and step back slightly so the cable is taut.

Hinge slightly forward at the hips so your torso leans about 10–20 degrees; keep knees soft and maintain a neutral spine. Arms should be extended in front of you with a small, fixed bend in the elbows (not fully locked).

Initiate the rep by driving the shoulders into extension: press the hands downward and back in a controlled arc until the hands reach thigh level and you feel maximal contraction in the lats. Keep the elbows stationary and avoid bending them to pull.
Pause briefly at the bottom while squeezing the lats and maintaining straight-arm tension.
Slowly return the handle to the starting position under control, keeping constant tension on the cable and avoiding letting the shoulders fully relax at the top.
Breathe out during the downward (working) phase and inhale while returning to the start. Perform desired number of reps with steady tempo.
exercises.description: Standing straight-arm cable pushdowns target the lats by driving the arms down from a high cable with minimal elbow flexion. The movement emphasizes shoulder extension and scapular retraction while keeping the torso stable.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Every working set should reach true muscular failure.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Pronated to Neutral Grip Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips, push your butt back and keep a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (or as far as your mobility allows); keep the spine neutral and neck aligned.
instructions: Stand with feet hip‑width apart holding a dumbbell in each hand with a pronated (palms facing your thighs/ground) grip.

Hinge at the hips, push your butt back and keep a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (or as far as your mobility allows); keep the spine neutral and neck aligned.
Let the dumbbells hang straight down from your shoulders with arms extended and core braced.
Initiate the row by pulling your elbows back and up, driving them toward your hips/ribcage and squeezing your shoulder blades together.
As you pull, rotate each wrist from a pronated position toward a neutral (palms facing each other) position so the top of the movement finishes with a neutral grip.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining scapular squeeze, then slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting pronated hang while controlling wrist rotation back to pronation.
Breathe out as you pull, inhale as you lower; keep the torso stable (avoid using momentum or excessive torso rotation).
Keep sets controlled and maintain a flat back throughout; reduce range or weight if you cannot keep spinal neutrality.
exercises.description: A two‑arm bent‑over dumbbell row where you start with a pronated (palms down) grip and rotate to a neutral grip as you pull; it trains the large pulling muscles of the back while engaging the arms and rear delts. Performed from a hip hinge position with a flat spine and a controlled pull and lower.

exercises.name: Cable Pulldown (pro lat bar)
exercises.notes: Select an appropriate weight and grasp the pro-lat bar with a wide, pronated (palms facing away) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width.
instructions: Sit on the pulldown bench with knees secured under the thigh pads and feet flat on the floor (seated, knees braced).
Select an appropriate weight and grasp the pro-lat bar with a wide, pronated (palms facing away) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width.
Begin tall with a neutral spine and a slight lean back from the hips (not from the lower back), chest lifted and eyes forward.
Inhale, then exhale and initiate the movement by pulling the shoulder blades down and together (scapular retraction) to engage the lats.
Pull the bar down in a controlled arc toward the upper chest (sternal area), leading with the elbows and keeping the wrists neutral.
Pause briefly at the bottom and squeeze the lats and upper back without shrugging the shoulders.
Inhale and slowly allow the bar to return to the start position with straight arms, fully extending the shoulders while maintaining a stable torso.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, avoiding excessive torso lean or momentum; keep movement controlled and driven by the lats.
exercises.description: Seated cable pulldown using a pro-lat bar performed from a tall seated position, pulling the bar down to the upper chest to target the latissimus dorsi and upper back. Focus is on scapular retraction and controlled eccentric return.

exercises.name: Cable Low Seated Row with V bar
exercises.notes: Sit tall with a slight forward lean from the hips, chest lifted, core engaged and shoulders relaxed; allow the arms to fully extend so the weight is supported by the arms and scapulae are slightly protracted.
instructions: Sit on the bench facing the low cable pulley with feet braced on the footplate and knees slightly bent; grasp the V‑bar with a neutral (palms facing) grip.

Sit tall with a slight forward lean from the hips, chest lifted, core engaged and shoulders relaxed; allow the arms to fully extend so the weight is supported by the arms and scapulae are slightly protracted.
Initiate the movement by pulling the elbows straight back toward your torso (not flaring wide), driving the elbows past your sides and pulling the V‑bar to your lower chest/upper abdomen.
At the end range, squeeze your shoulder blades together and hold the contraction for 1–2 seconds while keeping the torso stable and upright.
Slowly extend the arms forward in a controlled manner, allowing the scapulae to re‑protract and return to the start position without rounding the lower back.
Breathe out while pulling, inhale while returning; avoid using momentum or leaning excessively backward—keep the motion controlled and driven by the back and arms.
exercises.description: Seated low cable row using a V‑bar is a horizontal pulling exercise that emphasizes the middle/upper back by driving the elbows back and squeezing the shoulder blades, while also engaging the lats and arm muscles. The neutral V‑grip shortens the lever and increases biceps involvement.

exercises.name: Cable Standing Alternate Chest Fly
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to approximately chest height and attach single-hand handles.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to approximately chest height and attach single-hand handles.
Stand in the center of the cable machine in a staggered (split) stance with one foot forward and a slight bend in both knees; lean slightly forward from the hips to engage the core.
Grasp both handles with a neutral grip and step forward until there is tension in the cables; hold both arms out to the sides with a slight bend in the elbows and shoulders down and back.
Keep the torso stable and core braced; allow one handle to remain out while you perform the fly with the other arm.

Exhale and bring the working hand in a horizontal arc across your body toward the midline, finishing with the palm facing your opposite shoulder or hands meeting in front of the sternum; maintain that slight elbow bend throughout the movement.
Pause briefly at the peak contraction, squeezing the pectorals, then slowly reverse the motion under control until the arm returns to the start position.
Alternate to the other arm after completing the prescribed reps for the first arm, keeping the non-working arm either extended or lightly resisting to maintain symmetry and tension.
Maintain steady controlled tempo, avoid torso rotation or using momentum, and keep shoulders down (not shrugged) throughout the set.
exercises.description: A standing cable fly performed one arm at a time to isolate the chest by bringing a single handle across the body in a controlled arc while the other arm returns. Use a staggered stance and slight forward lean to maintain balance and core stability.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie back so you are supine on the bench, feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and maintain a neutral spine with a slight natural arch; retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together.
instructions: Sit on the edge of a flat bench with a dumbbell resting on each thigh.
Lie back so you are supine on the bench, feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and maintain a neutral spine with a slight natural arch; retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together.
Use your legs and core to help bring the dumbbells up to chest height so your upper arms are about 45 degrees from your torso and palms are facing forward (or slightly inward if preferred).

Inhale, then slowly lower the dumbbells in a controlled manner until your elbows are roughly level with the bench or just below chest level, keeping wrists neutral and elbows tracking slightly tucked.

Pause briefly at the bottom without relaxing the shoulder blade position, then exhale and press the dumbbells upward and slightly together until your arms are almost fully extended — avoid locking the elbows.
Control the descent back to the starting position and repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
To finish the set, bring the dumbbells safely to your knees, sit up, and set them down or have a spotter assist if using heavy weight.
exercises.description: The dumbbell bench press is a supine pressing movement performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and delts for elbow and shoulder extension. Dumbbells allow a greater range of motion and require more stabilization from the forearms and serratus anterior.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Decline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set a decline bench to a moderate decline (about 15–30 degrees) and choose a pair of dumbbells you can control for the desired reps.
instructions: Set a decline bench to a moderate decline (about 15–30 degrees) and choose a pair of dumbbells you can control for the desired reps.

Sit on the high end of the bench, place the dumbbells on your thighs, hook your feet securely under the foot pads, then use your legs to help roll back onto the bench into a declined supine position with your head lower than your hips.
Plant your feet under the pads, keep your hips touching the bench, retract and slightly squeeze your shoulder blades together to create a stable chest position.
Bring the dumbbells up to chest level with a pronated grip (palms facing forward) and elbows at roughly a 30–45° angle from your torso; this is the start position.
Take a full breath in, then press the dumbbells upward and slightly together until your arms are nearly straight but not locked out; exhale as you press.
Pause briefly at the top with control, keeping wrists neutral and shoulders stable (avoid shrugging or flaring the elbows excessively).
Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly and under control back to chest level, stopping when the dumbbells are just above your lower chest or sternum—do not bounce off the chest.
Repeat for the prescribed number of reps while maintaining a tight core and steady foot contact under the pads; use a spotter for heavier sets or if fatigue compromises control.
After finishing, carefully bring the dumbbells to your thighs and use your legs to help sit up rather than twisting off the bench.
exercises.description: A decline dumbbell bench press is a weighted pressing movement performed on a decline bench that emphasizes the lower portion of the pectorals while also engaging the triceps and anterior delts. It is performed lying supine on a declined bench with feet secured under the pads and pressing dumbbells from chest level to full arm extension.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Fly
exercises.notes: Set an adjustable bench to a 30–45° incline and sit with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 30–45° incline and sit with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
Lie back so your head, upper back and glutes are supported (supine on an incline bench) and place your feet flat on the floor for stability.
Use your legs to help rack the dumbbells and press them overhead so your arms are extended above your chest, palms facing each other (neutral grip); keep a slight bend in the elbows.
Retract your shoulder blades slightly and maintain a neutral spine throughout the movement; avoid excessive lower-back arching.

Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells in a wide, controlled arc out to your sides until you feel a stretch in the upper chest—stop when your elbows are roughly in line with the bench or slightly below shoulder level.
Keep the elbows fixed at the same slight bend throughout the descent; do not let the elbows drop or the wrists bend excessively.
Exhale and bring the dumbbells back together over your chest by squeezing the pectorals, following the same arc path used on the way down.
Pause briefly at the top with the weights close together (but avoid slamming them), then repeat for the desired number of reps.
Use a lighter weight if you feel shoulder joint pain; focus on controlled range of motion and chest contraction rather than heavy load.
exercises.description: Lying on an incline bench, perform a wide-arc dumbbell fly to emphasize the upper portion of the chest; movement stretches the chest as you lower and contracts the pectorals as you bring the weights back together. Keep a slight bend in the elbows and move in a controlled arc to avoid shoulder strain.

exercises.name: Cable Low Fly
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single-handle grips; select a moderate weight.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single-handle grips; select a moderate weight.
Stand centered between the towers in a staggered or shoulder-width stance with knees soft and core braced (slight forward lean at the hips).
Reach down and grab each handle with a neutral or slightly supinated grip, palms facing in or up, elbows slightly bent and fixed in that position throughout the movement.
Begin with the arms out to the sides and low (handles near hips or knees) and inhale.
Exhale and pull the handles upward and inward in a wide arc, keeping the elbows slightly bent, bringing the hands together or in front of the chest/upper sternum.
Pause briefly at the peak contraction and actively squeeze the pectorals while avoiding excessive shoulder shrugging.
Slowly reverse the motion under control, allowing the cables to return the arms to the start position while maintaining tension in the chest.
Repeat for desired repetitions and re-rack safely; keep torso stable and avoid using momentum.
exercises.description: Standing cable low fly is a low-to-high cable chest fly that targets the pectorals by sweeping the arms upward and together from low pulley positions; performed standing with a slight forward lean and a soft bend in the elbows to emphasize the chest contraction.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Every working set should reach true muscular failure.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and knees bent at about 90 degrees.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and knees bent at about 90 degrees.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral grip (palms facing inward), arms fully relaxed.
Brace your core and press your lower back lightly into the bench to maintain a neutral spine throughout.
Slightly bend your elbows (soft arms) and keep the bend constant for the entire movement.
Exhale and raise both arms out to the sides in a controlled arc until your arms are roughly parallel to the floor (shoulder height); lead with the elbows and keep wrists neutral.
Keep shoulders down (do not shrug) and avoid leaning or using torso momentum—if you need to swing, reduce the weight.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts, then inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position.
Perform repetitions with a steady tempo (e.g., 1–2 seconds up, 1–2 seconds down) and choose a weight that allows strict form.
exercises.description: A seated lateral raise isolates the lateral head of the deltoid by lifting light-to-moderate dumbbells out to the sides from a seated, supported position. Use a slight elbow bend and controlled tempo to emphasize shoulder abduction and limit momentum.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Drive Front Raise
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly soft and core braced (standing position).
instructions: Stand upright with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly soft and core braced (standing position).
Hold a pair of dumbbells together in front of your thighs with a neutral or slightly pronated grip (palms facing each other or slightly down), elbows slightly bent.
Take a breath, then drive the dumbbells upward by contracting the front of the shoulders — lift to about shoulder or eye level with a controlled motion; avoid using your lower back to lean back.
Keep a small bend in the elbows throughout the lift and lead with the elbows rather than the hands.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining shoulder tension, then slowly lower the dumbbells back down to the starting position under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, focusing on minimal swinging and steady core engagement to stabilize the torso.
exercises.description: A standing front raise variation where you drive the dumbbells upward from the hips to shoulder/eye level to emphasize the anterior delts while challenging upper chest and trap stability. Performed with a controlled drive and slow descent to minimize momentum.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

exercises.name: EZ Bar Standing Front Raise
exercises.notes: Stand upright (standing) with feet about hip-width apart and knees slightly bent.
instructions: Stand upright (standing) with feet about hip-width apart and knees slightly bent.
Grip the EZ bar with a shoulder-width, overhand (palms down) grip using the inner bends of the bar so wrists are comfortable and neutral.
Let the bar hang in front of your thighs with arms extended and shoulders down and back; brace your core (abs) and keep a neutral spine.
Keeping a slight bend in the elbows, raise the bar straight forward in a smooth motion until your hands reach about shoulder height; lead with the elbows and avoid shrugging.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining shoulder tension and a stable torso; do not lean back or use momentum.
Slowly lower the bar back to the starting position with control, keeping the core braced throughout.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping the movement strict; avoid lifting the bar above shoulder level or swinging the hips.
exercises.description: Standing front raise performed with an EZ bar to isolate the anterior portion of the delts by lifting the weight straight in front of the body to shoulder height. The movement uses a controlled tempo to emphasize shoulder flexion while the core and upper back stabilize the torso.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Cuban Press
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees soft and core braced; hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral grip (palms facing your body).
instructions: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, knees soft and core braced; hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral grip (palms facing your body).
Begin the movement by driving your elbows up and out in a controlled upright-row motion, keeping the dumbbells close to the body until your elbows reach roughly shoulder height.

With elbows elevated, externally rotate at the shoulders so your forearms move forward and the dumbbells are brought in front of your face/chest area (upper arms should be roughly parallel to the floor).
From that externally rotated position, press the dumbbells straight overhead until your arms are fully extended, keeping the core tight and avoiding excessive back arch.

Lower the dumbbells back down to the externally rotated position in a controlled manner, then rotate the forearms back to the upright-row position and lower the weights to the starting position at your sides.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, using a weight that allows smooth, controlled rotation and full overhead lockout without shrugging the shoulders.
exercises.description: A standing compound shoulder movement that combines an upright row, external rotation, and overhead press to build deltoid strength and shoulder stability while recruiting the traps and triceps.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bent Over Face Pull
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips until your torso is bent forward (roughly 30–60° from horizontal), keep a slight bend in the knees and maintain a neutral spine and braced core (avoid rounding the lower back).
instructions: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart holding a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip (palms facing each other).
Hinge at the hips until your torso is bent forward (roughly 30–60° from horizontal), keep a slight bend in the knees and maintain a neutral spine and braced core (avoid rounding the lower back).
Let the arms hang straight down from the shoulders so the dumbbells are below chest level and relax the shoulder blades.
Initiate the movement by pulling the elbows up and out (away from the body) so the upper arms move roughly parallel to the floor; lead with the elbows rather than the hands.

As the elbows reach about shoulder height, externally rotate the shoulders so the dumbbells move toward the sides of your face (palms may end up facing you or slightly upward), and squeeze the rear delts and scapular muscles together at the top.
Pause briefly at the top for 1–2 seconds, keeping the torso still and chest facing the floor.
Slowly reverse the movement under control, allowing the arms to extend back to the starting hanging position while keeping the torso braced.
Breathe out during the pull and inhale as you lower; choose a weight that allows strict form and avoid using momentum or excessive torso movement.
exercises.description: A bent-over face pull performed with dumbbells that targets the rear delts by pulling the weights toward the face while hinging at the hips to emphasize posterior shoulder and upper-back activation. It combines a high-row with external rotation to hit the posterior shoulder and scapular muscles.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Alternate Front Raise
exercises.notes: Sit upright on the edge of a flat bench with feet flat on the floor about hip-width apart and a neutral spine.
instructions: Sit upright on the edge of a flat bench with feet flat on the floor about hip-width apart and a neutral spine.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a firm grip, arms fully extended and shoulders relaxed; palms can face your thighs (neutral) or rotate slightly so the thumb points up.
Brace your core and keep your chest up; avoid leaning back or using the legs to help lift.
Exhale and raise one dumbbell straight in front of you with a slight bend in the elbow, lifting to about shoulder height (arm roughly parallel to the floor).
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining a tall posture and keeping the opposite arm stationary at your side.
Inhale and slowly lower the weight back to the starting position with control.
Repeat the movement with the other arm, alternating sides in a steady, controlled rhythm; avoid swinging the torso or using momentum.
Keep repetitions controlled (2–3 seconds up, 2–3 seconds down) and stop the lift before your wrist or shoulder becomes painful; breathe out on the lift and in on the return.
exercises.description: Seated alternate front raises involve lifting one dumbbell at a time from the thigh to about shoulder height while seated, primarily working the anterior deltoid. Maintain an upright posture and controlled tempo to isolate the front delts and minimize momentum.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Bent Arm Lateral raise
exercises.notes: Hinge your torso forward about 30–45 degrees while keeping your back straight, chest up and neck neutral.
instructions: Sit on the front edge of a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and knees close together; hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms hanging down between your legs.
Hinge your torso forward about 30–45 degrees while keeping your back straight, chest up and neck neutral.
Bend your elbows to about 90 degrees and keep your forearms nearly vertical with palms facing each other (neutral grip).
Exhale and lift the dumbbells out to your sides by raising your elbows, leading with the elbows until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor (shoulder height).
Pause briefly at the top, maintaining the bent-elbow position and avoiding shrugging the shoulders.
Inhale and slowly lower the weights back to the starting position under control, keeping the torso still and avoiding swinging.
Repeat for the desired number of reps; choose a weight that allows controlled motion and focus on feeling the delts work rather than using momentum.
exercises.description: Seated bent-arm lateral raise is a shoulder isolation exercise performed seated with the torso tipped forward and elbows bent, lifting the dumbbells out to the sides to emphasize the lateral deltoids. It minimizes momentum and places more emphasis on the delts and upper scapular muscles.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

workout.title: Shoulders/Arms - BODYBUILD/ENDURANCE

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Front and Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Stand upright (standing) with feet about hip-to-shoulder width, knees soft and core braced; hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral grip.
instructions: Stand upright (standing) with feet about hip-to-shoulder width, knees soft and core braced; hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral grip.
Keep a small bend in the elbows and maintain a neutral wrist; shoulders down and back (do not shrug) to avoid excess trap activation.
Front raise: exhale and lift both dumbbells straight forward with controlled motion until your arms are roughly parallel to the floor (about shoulder height), palms facing down or slightly inward.
Pause briefly at the top (0–1 second), then inhale and lower the weights slowly back to the starting position under control.

Immediately perform the lateral raise: exhale and lift both dumbbells out to your sides with the same slight elbow bend, leading with the elbow, raising to shoulder height so the arms form a T with your torso.
Hold briefly at the top, then inhale and lower the weights slowly back to your sides with control, avoiding swinging or using momentum.
Repeat the sequence (front raise then lateral raise) for the desired number of repetitions, keeping core engaged and torso still throughout.

Progression/cues: use light-to-moderate weight to maintain strict form, keep movements slow (about 1–2 seconds up, 2–3 seconds down), and avoid elevating the shoulders toward the ears to limit excessive trap involvement.
exercises.description: A standing dumbbell combination that first raises the weights in front of the body (front raise) then out to the sides (lateral raise) to train the shoulder delts. Use controlled movement and a slight elbow bend to isolate the delts and minimize momentum.

exercises.name: EZ Bar Standing Front Raise
exercises.notes: Stand upright (standing) with feet about hip-width apart and knees slightly bent.
instructions: Stand upright (standing) with feet about hip-width apart and knees slightly bent.
Grip the EZ bar with a shoulder-width, overhand (palms down) grip using the inner bends of the bar so wrists are comfortable and neutral.
Let the bar hang in front of your thighs with arms extended and shoulders down and back; brace your core (abs) and keep a neutral spine.
Keeping a slight bend in the elbows, raise the bar straight forward in a smooth motion until your hands reach about shoulder height; lead with the elbows and avoid shrugging.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining shoulder tension and a stable torso; do not lean back or use momentum.
Slowly lower the bar back to the starting position with control, keeping the core braced throughout.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping the movement strict; avoid lifting the bar above shoulder level or swinging the hips.
exercises.description: Standing front raise performed with an EZ bar to isolate the anterior portion of the delts by lifting the weight straight in front of the body to shoulder height. The movement uses a controlled tempo to emphasize shoulder flexion while the core and upper back stabilize the torso.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

exercises.name: Dumbbell One Arm Shoulder Press
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet about hip-width apart and knees soft; hold a dumbbell in one hand at shoulder height with the elbow bent and the palm facing forward or slightly inward.
instructions: Stand tall with feet about hip-width apart and knees soft; hold a dumbbell in one hand at shoulder height with the elbow bent and the palm facing forward or slightly inward.
Brace your core and keep a neutral spine; place the free hand on your hip or at your side for balance.
Exhale and press the dumbbell straight upward by extending the elbow and lifting the arm until it is fully or nearly fully overhead without overarching the lower back.
Keep the shoulder blade stable and avoid shrugging the working shoulder; do not lean or rotate the torso toward the working side.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbell under control back to the starting shoulder-height position.
Perform the desired number of reps on one side, then switch arms and repeat. Use a lighter weight if you cannot maintain good posture or if the lower back arches.
exercises.description: Standing single-arm dumbbell shoulder press performed from shoulder height to overhead to target the delts. The movement uses the triceps and serratus anterior for elbow extension and scapular stability while the core resists trunk tilt.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Shoulder Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with elbows bent ~90°, palms facing forward or slightly inward and wrists neutral.
Inhale, keep the spine neutral and shoulders down away from the ears.
Exhale and press both dumbbells upward in a controlled motion until the arms are nearly fully extended overhead (avoid locking the elbows).
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts and keeping the torso stable.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to shoulder height with control to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining a steady tempo and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Use a lighter weight or a spotter if form breaks down or if pressing heavy loads.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell shoulder press is a compound pressing movement performed from a seated, back‑supported position that primarily develops the delts while also engaging the triceps and upper back for stabilization.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Rear Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench so the back pad is at a low angle and stand facing the side of the bench.
instructions: Set an incline bench so the back pad is at a low angle and stand facing the side of the bench.
Pick up a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral (palms facing) grip and take a hip-width stance with a slight bend in the knees.
Hinge forward at the hips and place your forehead or chest gently against the bench pad for support so your torso is close to horizontal (bench-assisted bent-over position).
Let the arms hang straight down from the shoulders with a small bend in the elbows; keep a neutral spine and the head supported on the bench.
Exhale and raise the dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc — lead with the elbows — until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor and you feel the rear delts contract.
Pause briefly at the top, then inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly and under control back to the starting position.
Keep the movement strict (no torso rotation or momentum) and maintain head/chest support on the bench throughout the set.
Choose a weight that allows controlled form for the full range of motion and perform recommended reps.
exercises.description: A bent-over, bench-supported dumbbell raise that targets the rear (posterior) delts by moving the arms out to the sides while the torso is stabilized. The head/chest is supported on an incline bench to reduce momentum and isolate the rear deltoids.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
